title: 'I'm still watching my World Cup goal' - Isidor on his incredible strike
description: Haiti may not have won a point at the World Cup but Sunderland's Wilson Isidor will never forget the feeling of scoring for his country at the tournament.  Isidor's strike from outside the box against Morocco was one of the contenders for goal of the tournament and, speaking in Nashville before Sunderland's friendly against Liverpool this weekend, the 25-year-old admitted that, even a month on, he still thinks about that strike.  Regis le Bris' Sunderland squad travelled to the United States on Wednesday, with Isidor joining up with his team-mates having stayed in the country after the World Cup.
fullText:
Haiti may not have won a point at the World Cup but Sunderland's Wilson Isidor will never forget the feeling of scoring for his country at the tournament.

Isidor's strike from outside the box against Morocco was one of the contenders for goal of the tournament and, speaking in Nashville before Sunderland's friendly against Liverpool this weekend, the 25-year-old admitted that, even a month on, he still thinks about that strike.

"I've looked at it almost a thousand times, probably more. I think about it every day and people remind me every day too. It was a great moment," Isidor told BBC Sport.

"We know the Haitian people were waiting for some emotion at the World Cup. When I saw the celebrations in the country, it made me so emotional. It's the greatest moment of my career so far."

Regis le Bris' Sunderland squad travelled to the United States on Wednesday, with Isidor joining up with his team-mates having stayed in the country after the World Cup.

When asked about Saturday's encounter with Liverpool, who have a new head coach in Andoni Iraola, Isidor said: "If somebody says to me that the game on Saturday is friendly, he's lying.

"It's not possible to have a friendly game when you play Liverpool. We'll give everything."

The 25-year-old has been with the Black Cats since 2024, is eager to push on after Sunderland qualified for the Europa League by finishing seventh in the Premier League.

"The first year in the Premier League was kind of ok with six goals," said Isidor, who joined Sunderland from Zenit St Petersburg two years ago. "Now I want to score more than six goals because I've tasted it.

"I was in a bad situation not playing in Russia. He [Le Bris] trusted me in the moment when no-one trusted me, so I will remember him for life."

title: WNBA All-Star skills participants: Full lineups for 3-Point Contest & Shooting Stars in 2026
description: Here's a look at who will be taking center stage in Chicago this weekend.
fullText:
WNBA All-Star skills participants: Full lineups for 3-Point Contest & Shooting Stars in 2026 originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

The WNBA All-Star Weekend is here as the best players in the league congregate in Chicago, Illinois, to show off their skills. Before the game itself takes place, select players will participate in the 3-point contest and the shooting stars on Friday night.

Since 2017, only four players have won the 3-Point Contest, with Allie Quigley winning the event four times. Sabrina Ionescu won her second contest in three years last season. Instead of the Skills Challenge, though, the WNBA is going with a Shooting Stars event featuring four former legends and four high school stars.

This year there will be a lot of new faces front and center for the WNBA All-Star weekend. Here's a look at who will be taking centre stage in Chicago this weekend.

SN's WNBA HQ: Live WNBA scores | Updated WNBA standings | Full WNBA schedule

Six players will participate in this year's 3-Point Contest, including No. 1 overall pick Azzi Fudd. The former UConn star is shooting 38.8 percent from deep on the season and will be busy experiencing her first All-Star Weekend.

The two best players in the field are Marina Mabrey and Rhyne Howard, as the guards are currently first and second in 3-pointers made per game this year. Mabrey's 3.3 3-pointers per game leads the league, with Howard's 3.2 3-pointers per game sitting at second, and no other player in the WNBA has more than 2.7.

Carleton, Salaun and Hiedeman are strong 3-point shooters who round out the field, as each of the three is in the top 10 in 3-pointers made per game.

This year, the WNBA is replacing a skills challenge with a Shooting Stars challenge, bringing together former legends with current players from the same franchise. The Minnesota Lynx, Seattle Storm, Washington Mystics and Dallas Wings will all be represented in the event.

Minnesota is bringing back Rebekkah Brunson to pair with Courtney Williams, wile Seattle will have Lauren Jackson back alongside Flau'jae Johnson. For Washington, Elena Delle Donne will join Shakira Austin, and the Dallas Wings will have Jessica Shepard alongside Detroit Shock legend Deanna "Tweety" Nolan.

Additionally, each team will be assigned a third player on their team, as four high school players from Nike EYBL will also participate. According to the WNBA website, those four players are :

The WNBA All-Star break will take place from July 23-27, with events taking place on July 24 and 25 live from the United Center in Chicago, Illinois. The first night will feature the 3-Point Contest and Shooting Stars, while the second night will be the All-Star Game.

title: Fiorentina remain interested in Dovbyk as Kean alternative
description: Fiorentina is already considering its next transfer market moves and evaluating possible solutions to strengthen its attack. The future of the Viola attack will depend primarily on Moise Kean, who cou...
fullText:
Fiorentina is already considering its next transfer market moves and evaluating possible solutions to strengthen its attack.

The future of the Viola attack will depend primarily on Moise Kean, who could leave Florence for an offer close to €40 million.

Compared to last summer, the Tuscan club is more confident about the striker’s possible departure. Kean, in fact, is coming off a decidedly less impressive season than the 2024/25 season, in which he scored a total of 25 goals across league and cup competitions.

As reported in today’s edition of La Gazzetta dello Sport, should the striker move to Como or another interested club, Fiorentina could turn its attention to Artem Dovbyk.

The Ukrainian striker is considered on his way out of Roma and could be sold permanently for a fee under €20 million, with the Giallorossi also prepared to record a small capital loss.

His salary of approximately €3.5 million represents a significant investment, but remains lower than the €4.5 million Kean earns, although the latter is eligible for the Growth Decree.

Dovbyk, 29, comes from a season that ended with just three goals and could seek a revival under the guidance of Fabio Grosso, returning to the levels shown at Girona, where he reached 24 goals.

title: Vicario chased by Juventus after being left out of Spurs' tour squad
description: Juventus are expected to step up their pursuit of Guglielmo Vicario after the goalkeeper was left out of Tottenham’s pre-season tour squad, the Press Association understands. Vicario was not included in Spurs’ 35-man touring party which started the journey to New Zealand on Thursday.
fullText:
Juventus are expected to step up their pursuit of Guglielmo Vicario after the goalkeeper was left out of Tottenham’s pre-season tour squad, the Press Association understands.

Vicario was not included in Spurs’ 35-man touring party which started the journey to New Zealand on Thursday. A club statement confirmed a “minor injury” meant the Italy international stayed back in England.

However, after Vicario lost his first-choice role to Antonin Kinsky at the end of a difficult 2025-26 campaign, the 29-year-old had already received the green light to leave and his absence from Tottenham’s pre-season tour will only offer further encouragement to Juventus.

Juventus had placed Argentina and Aston Villa goalkeeper Emi Martinez at the top of their wish list, but Villa director of football operations Damian Vidagany revealed earlier this month that Martinez would not be sold.

After more advances for Martinez failed, PA understands Juventus are ready to move onto alternative options and it will open the door for Vicario to bring his three-year stay at Spurs to an end.

Vicario joined in 2023 from Empoli and immediately established himself as number one under Ange Postecoglou, but experienced a decline in form last season and even after he returned to fitness following a minor hernia procedure, Kinsky retained his starting berth under Roberto De Zerbi.

With experienced goalkeeper Martin Dubravka signed to be the understudy to Kinsky, who signed a new deal last month, Vicario was always expected to depart Tottenham in this transfer window and Juventus are now his most likely destination after Inter Milan had initially led the race.

PA understands the preference of Juventus would be a loan with an option to buy Vicario rather than an obligation, which may provide an obstacle between the clubs, but a conditional obligation is one potential solution.

description: Roma continues to work on outgoing transfers, aiming to generate the capital gains needed to finance incoming deals. Among the players who could leave the capital during the summer transfer window is ...
fullText:
Roma continues to work on outgoing transfers, aiming to generate the capital gains needed to finance incoming deals.

Among the players who could leave the capital during the summer transfer window is Manu Koné, one of the Giallorossi’s most sought-after players.

According to today’s edition of Il Tempo, the French midfielder has been firmly in Manchester United’s sights. The English club has expressed strong interest in him, but has yet to submit an official offer to Roma. The Roman club would only consider a sale if the offer exceeded €50 million.

This figure would allow the Giallorossi to generate a significant capital gain and increase the resources available to sporting director Tony D’Amico. Koné has reportedly already expressed openness to the possibility of a move to the Premier League, a league he considers particularly attractive.

The Frenchman, however, continues to hope for a call from Paris Saint-Germain.

The Parisian club, at least so far, has not yet expressed any concrete interest. Manchester United therefore remains the club most closely monitoring the situation, even though they have yet to make a move.

title: The Hundred: New era, familiar result for London Spirit as Liam Livingstone heroics fall short
description: Liam Livingstone produced a superb all-round display with 69 off 40 balls and 2 for 14, but it wasn't enough as London Spirit slipped to a seven-run defeat against Manchester Super Giants in their Hundred opener. Spirit's women also lost by seven wickets, making it a disappointing start to the new era at Lord's.
fullText:
TimesofIndia.com in London: New owners, new coaches, new players and a new captain. London Spirit arrived at Lord's on Thursday promising a fresh start, but their opening day of The Hundred 2026 produced a painfully familiar result.

For the fifth time in six seasons, Spirit's men began their campaign with defeat as Manchester Super Giants edged a thriller by seven runs in front of 23,847 spectators. Earlier, Spirit's women had been comprehensively beaten by seven wickets, completing a double defeat for the hosts.

In between came a distinctly Hundred-style interlude, with rapper Tinie Tempah performing amid jets of fire and even venturing into the Lord's crowd. But once attention returned to the cricket, it was Liam Livingstone who provided the evening's real entertainment.

Spirit's new captain almost single-handedly dragged his side towards victory with an extraordinary all-round performance.

Having put Manchester in, Spirit were initially made to toil by Tim Seifert, who marked his first Hundred appearance with 67 from 45 balls. The New Zealander added 49 with Jos Buttler (16) and 40 with Heinrich Klaasen (17) as the visitors reached 103 for two.

That was when Livingstone took matters into his own hands.

The captain bowled 20 of the final 25 deliveries, conceding only 14 runs and taking two wickets as Manchester spectacularly lost seven wickets for 35 runs. Six of those fell for just 22 runs in the final 26 balls, leaving the visitors on 138 for nine.

On a Lord's surface where batters struggled for timing, only four days after England and India had combined for 747 runs in an ODI, Manchester's total appeared around par.

Spirit's chase quickly ran into trouble when James Rew departed for a two-ball duck. Livingstone rebuilt alongside Kiran Carlson (19) before adding 68 with Dewald Brevis, although the South African's 22 came from 23 balls as the required rate climbed.

With 56 needed from 30 balls, Livingstone shifted gears.

He took 12 from Noor Ahmad's final five deliveries and brought up his half-century from 32 balls with a trademark slog sweep. Manchester also gave him a helping hand, most notably when Seifert spilled a chance at deep midwicket and watched the ball bounce over the boundary for six.

Suddenly, Spirit were back in it.

They required 21 from the final 10 deliveries, but Manchester's England pace duo held their nerve. Gus Atkinson removed Brevis with a caught-and-bowled dismissal and finished with figures of two for 15, leaving Sonny Baker to defend 12 from the final five balls.

Baker had conceded 25 runs from his first 15 deliveries, while Livingstone had already struck five sixes.

This time, however, the 23-year-old seamer won the battle. After conceding three runs from his first two deliveries, Baker went full and fast and crashed into Livingstone's stumps. James Coles was caught off the final ball as Spirit finished on 131 for six.

Livingstone's 69 from 40 balls, coupled with figures of two for 14, earned him the Match Hero award but not the result.

"We bowled some really good stuff through the middle, dragged it back towards the end, and we nearly chased it down," Livingstone said. "We just got a bit too far behind the rate to catch up in the end."

There was considerably less drama in the women's game.

Grace Harris struck the opening delivery for four, but Spirit promptly collapsed to 23 for four in the powerplay and were eventually bowled out for 80 in 91 balls.

Manchester needed only 59 deliveries to complete a seven-wicket win, with Paige Scholfield finishing the chase with successive four and six.

For London Spirit, it was a sobering beginning to what was supposed to be a fresh chapter. Livingstone believes there is something to build on, and his own performance certainly provided encouragement.

But for all the changes around Lord's, opening night brought the same old outcome: two games, two defeats.

title: Real Madrid eye RB Leipzig star as Michael Olise alternative
description: There is no doubt that the French forward Michael Olise could be the perfect signing for Real Madrid this summer. The Merengues already have two superstars playing in their attacking line, i.e., Vinic...
fullText:
There is no doubt that the French forward Michael Olise could be the perfect signing for Real Madrid this summer. The Merengues already have two superstars playing in their attacking line, i.e., Vinicius Junior and Kylian Mbappe, and the arrival of Olise would make the forward line one of the most fearsome in Europe, at least on paper.

However, it is very unlikely that Los Blancos will get the Frenchman this summer. This is mainly because his club, Bayern Munich, have no plans to part with him, and will do everything to ensure he stays in their ranks.

In such a context, Real Madrid have apparently set their sights on another intruiging target. SPORT cites the German outlet Bild to claim that the Merengues are seeing Yan Diomande as an alternative to Olise, and are willing to pay €100 million for his signing.

The 19-year-old Ivorian player has attracted the interest of several big clubs across Europe after having impressed in his first season in Bundesliga, during which he scored 13 goals and provided 9 assists across 36 appearances. He also impressed with Ivory Coast during the World Cup 2026.

Premier League sides like Liverpool, Arsenal, and Manchester City are all ready to pay a similar amount for him, but for the moment, it is the Ligue 1 side Paris Saint-Germain that are leading the race for his signature. The Parisians have already reached an agreement with the player, but still need to negotiate with RB Leipzig, who are unwilling to let the player leave for less than €120 million. A €100 million offer from Liverpool was rejected by the German side immediately.

Yan Diomande has already played in La Liga, with Leganes, and made his debut against Real Madrid on March 29, 2025. For now, he is still on vacation after the World Cup, and Real Madrid, along with a host of other clubs, remain attentive to how his potential transfer to PSG unfolds.

title: Man Utd mascot, six, hit by club's ticket tout crackdown
description: Ruari's mum denies any wrongdoing and says she manages other family members' tickets for them.
fullText:
A mother has said her six-year-old son has had restrictions put on his Manchester United season ticket as part of the club's anti-touting crackdown.

Katie, 39, from Urmston, Trafford, said she had been left unable to apply for an away game ticket for her "football daft" son Ruari - who had once been a match mascot - ahead of the Premier League opener against Hull.

In an email seen by the BBC, the club said repeated access to Ruairi's account, alongside an "extremely high" number of other ticketing accounts, indicated they had been centrally controlled.

But Katie - who said her appeal to the club had been turned down - said she had just helped other family members to use their accounts and had done nothing wrong.

Ruari is among "thousands" of fans who have had their accounts restricted by the club in a wider operation aimed at identifying potential ticket touts.

Man Utd previously said it was confident the "vast majority" of accounts identified for misuse had been correctly flagged, but acknowledged some supporters who were doing nothing wrong might also have been affected.

Katie said she had lodged a further appeal with the club's appeals panel, but Ruari's account remains restricted.

Katie said Ruairi had held a season ticket since birth and regularly attended home, away and European matches with his family.

Supporting Man Utd was "all we do", she said, adding that she met her husband at Old Trafford and had held her own season ticket for nearly 20 years, along with her late mother and siblings.

"Since [Ruari] could walk, he's had a ball at his feet," she said. "He's just football daft."

Katie said Ruairi had been a United mascot at Crystal Palace last season and had travelled to watch the team in Seville, Istanbul and Bilbao.

A post on X, in which Katie said the restriction would "break [Ruari's] heart", has been viewed more than 1.3 million times.

She said she had not told him about the restriction because "you can't put that on a six-year-old".

"The first game of the season, we're definitely going to miss," she said.

"I looked at his dad and said, 'How can you take that away from him?'"

Katie said 19 relatives and friends had formed a group so they could sit together in a singing section at Old Trafford.

She said she handled much of their ticket administration, including away applications, while family members helped one another manage accounts using shared devices and internet connections.

Some relatives were less confident using online ticketing systems, she said.

Katie's appeal challenges restrictions on six family accounts.

In it, she said no tickets had been sold for profit, advertised or supplied to unauthorised third parties, and no automated software, bots or scripts had been used.

She said that when family members had been unable to attend one or two matches, their tickets had been forwarded through Man Utd's official system.

The club's decision email said applying with other supporters and using its official forwarding process had not, by itself, been treated as evidence of wrongdoing.

However, it said the "scale, frequency and pattern" of the wider activity had not been satisfactorily explained.

The club concluded that Ruairi's account and its associated tickets had been put under another person's control and used or managed outside its authorised processes.

The email did not expressly accuse the family of reselling tickets or using bots.

Man Utd told Katie on the afternoon of 21 July that the restriction would remain, about four hours after she first sent her explanation.

The email said the decision followed a "detailed review" of the information she provided and the club's investigation.

Katie said she believed it was an "AI automated rejection" because it arrived so quickly and appeared to use the same wording as responses received by other supporters.

Man Utd declined to comment on Ruari's case.

But the club said similar anti-touting work last season recovered more than 14,000 tickets worth more than £1m and returned them to supporters through official channels.

title: Chelsea close in on Maxence Lacroix as Crystal Palace defender prepares for medical
description: Chelsea are closing in on the signing of Crystal Palace defender Maxence Lacroix, with the France international set to undergo a medical before completing his move to Stamford Bridge.The Blues have ag...
fullText:
Chelsea are closing in on the signing of Crystal Palace defender Maxence Lacroix, with the France international set to undergo a medical before completing his move to Stamford Bridge.

The Blues have agreed a deal worth around £55 million for the 26-year-old, who is expected to sign a long-term contract until 2032.

Lacroix has been one of Chelsea’s leading defensive targets throughout the summer as head coach Xabi Alonso looks to reshape his squad ahead of the new Premier League season.

The former Wolfsburg centre-back has established himself as one of the Premier League’s standout defenders since joining Crystal Palace in 2024.

He made 55 appearances in all competitions last season and played a pivotal role in one of the most successful campaigns in Palace’s history.

Lacroix helped Oliver Glasner’s side lift the FA Cup before adding the UEFA Conference League and Community Shield, cementing his reputation as one of the division’s most consistent central defenders.

His performances also earned him a place in France’s World Cup squad, where he featured three times during the tournament.

Chelsea believe his pace, physicality and ability to defend aggressively suit Alonso’s preferred style, with the club eager to strengthen a back line that has undergone significant changes this summer.

The arrival of Lacroix is also expected to intensify competition for places in central defence.

Chelsea are open to offers for several defenders before the transfer window closes, with Axel Disasi, Benoit Badiashile and Trevoh Chalobah among those whose futures remain uncertain.

Crystal Palace have already begun planning for life after Lacroix.

Augsburg defender Chrislain Matsima has emerged as the club’s preferred replacement, although Trabzonspor’s Chibuike Nwaiwu is also being monitored should negotiations fail to progress.

Lacroix joined Palace from Wolfsburg for around £15 million two years ago and has almost quadrupled his market value through a series of outstanding displays in south London.

Chelsea view the transfer as a major investment in a defender entering the prime years of his career.

Provided the medical is completed without any issues, an official announcement is expected in the coming days.

Chelsea will hope Lacroix’s arrival provides another important building block as Alonso looks to return the club to regular Premier League title contention after an eventful year of change both on and off the pitch.

title: Sky Sports: Arsenal Chase Two England Stars After Key Injury
description: Arsenal Transfer News: John Stones and Ezri Konsa Interest Makes Sense After William Saliba Injury BlowArsenal have a problem, and it is a serious one. William Saliba, arguably the club’s most impor...
fullText:
Arsenal have a problem, and it is a serious one. William Saliba, arguably the club’s most important defender, is set for a long spell out after suffering a back injury during France’s World Cup semi-final defeat to Spain. According to Sky Sports, Arsenal are now looking hard at the centre-back market, with John Stones and Ezri Konsa among the names under consideration.

That much is logical. Saliba has “avoided the need for surgery” but still faces “a lengthy period of rehabilitation”. You do not lose a defender of that calibre and pretend your existing depth solves everything. Arsenal have numbers at the back, yes, but top-level sides do not build plans around wishful thinking. They build around certainty, availability and profile.

That is where this story gets interesting. Stones and Konsa are both credible options, both known quantities in the Premier League, and both capable of playing at right-back. The similarity ends there.

Arsenal have already moved to “consolidated their defensive options” by signing Piero Hincapie permanently after his loan from Bayer Leverkusen. They also have Gabriel, Riccardo Calafiori, Cristhian Mosquera, Jurrien Timber and Ben White available, or close to available, across the defensive line.

But context matters. White is only just back in training after the knee injury he suffered in May. Timber has said he is “hopefully back soon” as he recovers from a groin problem. In other words, there are still too many variables. Arteta cannot go into the season knowing Saliba is out for an “extended period” and simply hope the rest of the puzzle pieces stay in place.

That is why the search is centred on players who can handle major minutes quickly, understand positional discipline, and slot into a side that asks defenders to play high, defend space and circulate possession cleanly.

Start with Stones. He is a free agent, which immediately changes the discussion. There is no transfer fee, no drawn-out negotiation with Manchester City, and no need to justify a huge outlay on a player in his thirties. If Arsenal want experienced short-term cover, he is sitting there.

There is also the Arteta factor. He knows the player well from his time at City and has never hidden his admiration. “John is a player I really like,” Arteta said previously. “We followed him before we signed him when I was at City. I worked with him for many years. I know him well and when I look at centre-backs, he has many attributes that I like.”

That quote matters because it tells you this is not casual admiration. Arteta understands exactly what Stones offers, composure on the ball, tactical intelligence, comfort stepping into midfield, and the kind of senior presence that can settle a back line. On a free, that has obvious appeal.

But there is no point dressing up the downside. Stones is 32 and his recent injury record is poor. He managed only 439 Premier League minutes last season, with thigh, calf and muscular issues disrupting him, and only 547 minutes the season before. Those are not minor caveats, they are central facts. Arsenal need cover for an injured defender. Signing another defender with recurring fitness concerns may be cheap, but cheap and sensible are not always the same thing.

Still, if the club’s thinking is to buy time, add experience and avoid spending heavily in a difficult market, Stones is a perfectly rational option.

Konsa is the cleaner football fit. He is younger at 28, more durable, and far more likely to handle the weekly physical demands of a Premier League title race. As reported by Sky Sports, Arsenal have been “exploring the cost of a potential deal for Konsa” but “there is a significant gap in valuation with Villa”. That is the issue in one sentence.

Villa are said to value him at £60m. For a player turning 29 in October, that is a serious fee. It may even be a prohibitive one, depending on the rest of Arsenal’s summer plans.

From a tactical standpoint, however, the attraction is obvious. Konsa plays primarily as a right-sided centre-back, the same side Saliba occupies, and he can cover right-back too. He is reliable in possession, calm under pressure and physically strong. More importantly, he stays fit. He has made at least 33 Premier League starts in five straight seasons, which in itself tells you plenty.

The underlying numbers strengthen the case. According to Opta, Konsa posted the highest ground-duel success rate of any outfield player in the Premier League last season at 73 per cent. That is elite output. Arsenal do not only need a body, they need someone who can defend space aggressively and win his individual battles. Konsa does that.

So yes, he is more expensive than Stones. He should be. He is also a better medium-term answer.

One interesting detail in the reporting is that neither Stones nor Konsa ranks among the 10 most stylistically similar players to Saliba using Twenty3’s similarity tool, although Konsa comes close in 12th. That should not be overstated, but it is worth noting. Replacing Saliba directly is difficult because there are not many defenders with his blend of recovery pace, passing range, composure and authority.

Arsenal are therefore making a compromise, whichever route they take. Stones offers intelligence, pedigree and a free deal, but obvious durability concerns. Konsa offers availability, athleticism and Premier League-tested defending, but at a price Aston Villa have little reason to lower.

There is no magic solution here. If Arsenal want the low-cost, short-term answer, Stones makes sense. If they want the stronger all-round fit for the next two or three seasons, Konsa is the better target. The catch is that one costs wages and risk, the other costs around £60m.

That is the market. You either pay with money or pay with uncertainty. Arsenal now need to choose which bill they are more comfortable settling.

From an Arsenal supporter’s point of view, this feels pretty simple. Saliba being out for a while is bad news, proper bad news, because he is the one defender you do not really replace. You just try to cope without him and make the drop-off as small as possible.

Stones is one of those deals where you instantly see why it might happen. Free transfer, huge experience, knows Arteta, can play football properly. Fine. No issue with that, as long as everyone is honest about the risk. If he misses chunks of the season, nobody can act surprised. The evidence is already there.

Konsa feels safer. Less glamorous, maybe, but probably more useful over the course of a full season. He plays, he defends, he knows the league, and he looks like someone who would slot in without drama. If Villa really want £60m, though, you can understand Arsenal hesitating, especially if there are other positions to sort out.

Personally, the ideal outcome might be the practical one. If the club believe Saliba will be back in a reasonable timeframe, Stones on a smart deal could do the job. If the absence is likely to drag and Arsenal want real security, then they should push harder for Konsa. Either way, doing nothing would be the worst move of the lot.

title: “Over the moon”: Elliot Anderson joins Manchester City in £116m move to the “Kings of Manchester”
description: Elliot Anderson has said he is over the moon to have signed for the Kings of Manchester after completing his move to Manchester City.Anderson has signed for Manchester City from Nottingham Forest in a...
fullText:
Elliot Anderson has said he is over the moon to have signed for the Kings of Manchester after completing his move to Manchester City.

Anderson has signed for Manchester City from Nottingham Forest in a £116m club record deal, with the midfielder signing a long-term contract at the Etihad.

Anderson was part of the England team that finished third at the 2026 World Cup this summer, a campaign that followed an impressivePremier League season. He ranked second in the Premier League for duels won among central midfielders, and third for forward passes and accurate crosses.

The 23-year-old was unveiled as a City player on Thursday evening and said he's delighted to have signed for a side with a relentless drive for trophies.

“I’m over the moon. I can’t wait to play for this Club. I’m really looking forward to it,”Anderson said.

“I think they’re winners, they’re relentless, and that’s the sort of team I want to be a part of - champions and people who win trophies.

“Over the last 10, 12 years, they’ve dominated, so that’s why I wanted to join.

“It’s happened quite quick [his career progression], but I think I always believed that I could get to this point.

“This is where I want to be. Now that I’m here, I’ll keep pushing and try to take this Club forward.”

Anderson has rival interest from Manchester United, who had been keen on a deal for the England international. However, he said he has joined the dominant side of the Mancunian rivalry.

“Yeah, it’s one of the biggest derbies in the world and for as long as I’ve known Manchester [football], City have been the Kings of Manchester,” he added.

“It’ll be a great fixture, and I can’t wait to be involved in something like that.”

title: Lewis Hamilton F1 Belgian GP penalty was completely justified, says former driver
description: Juan Pablo Montoya believes the FIA was right to penalise Lewis Hamilton for causing his opening-lap collision with George Russell at the Belgian GP
fullText:
Formula 1 race winner Juan Pablo Montoya has argued that the FIA stewards were completely justified in penalising Lewis Hamilton for his opening-lap collision with George Russell at the Belgian Grand Prix.

Russell, who started from third at Spa-Francorchamps, suffered a sudden loss of electrical deployment off the line. As he was swamped by the pack, he was caught up in a collision with Hamilton's Ferrari.

Hamilton suffered a snap of understeer and made contact with the Mercedes, sending Russell into the gravel and out of the race. The stewards subsequently handed the seven-time champion a five-second penalty for "causing a collision".

While many, including Russell, labelled the clash as a first-lap racing incident, Montoya argued that the fact it was the opening lap does not excuse a driver's failure to maintain their line.

"George was under control ahead of Lewis through the corner and gave Lewis enough room, and Lewis could not hold his line," Montoya explained during the F1 TV post-race show. "The reality of it is Lewis understeered into him. Was it on purpose? No, not at all.

"But it still caused the incident, and that's why I think George calls it a racing incident because it can happen on the first lap. I understand the way you see it, but the problem is the way the rules are written. That's a penalty."

Alex Brundle, who joined Montoya on the broadcast, partially agreed. "I think with George coming from behind, on any other lap, that is a penalisable incident," he said.

"On the first lap with everyone on cold tyres, we're going to differ across the panel here."

The retirement was a blow to Russell's 2026 title campaign, dropping him 50 points behind his Mercedes team-mate Kimi Antonelli. It was also Russell's second retirement of the season through no fault of his own.

title: Gymnast Amankwaah following in Fragapane's footsteps
description: Teenage gymnast Shantae Amankwaah on making her Commonwealth Games debut and following in Claudia Fragapane's footsteps.
fullText:
In 2014, when the Commonwealth Games were last in Glasgow, a little-known teenage gymnast, Claudia Fragapane, became one of Britain's breakthrough stars as she won four gold medals on her debut.

Twelve years on, with the competition returning to the same city, another Bristol-born teenager is hoping to one day follow in Fragapane's footsteps when she makes her own Games bow.

Shantae Amankwaah, who is part of the same Bristol Hawks gymnastics club where Fragapane began her career, said it is "surreal" to be heading to the same competition.

"I've always looked up to her. Even in 2014 I was only young, and I didn't go to this gym yet, but she had so many big achievements, and when I came, she was always someone I looked up to," Amankwaah told BBC Points West.

"It's really inspiring, especially because we're from the same place, the same area."

At 17, Amankwaah is the youngest gymnast in Team England's squad and she is set to compete in the uneven bars, beam and floor beginning on Friday.

In 2024 she was crowned British junior all-around champion, while earlier this year she won bronze at the British Championships in the senior women's all-around discipline.

Yet the Commonwealth Games marks a major step forward in Amankwaah's fledgling career, and, as such, the aim is simple: "Just take in the whole experience," Amankwaah said.

"Obviously it would be nice to get a medal, but just to really enjoy it with the team and have fun, because it only comes round every four years."

Fragapane retired in 2024, and her advice to Amankwaah was of a similar message.

"It's really difficult to just make the team, and I just wish her the best of luck," she said.

"She just needs to go out there and really enjoy herself and hold all the memories, because next thing she knows she could be sat here, 12 years later, thinking, 'Wow, where has the time gone?'"

The long-serving London nurse who ran for Ghana

As well as Fragapane, Amankwaah has plenty of support to call upon from her family, which is also packed with sporting talent.

Her grandmother Rose is a former sprinter who was once dubbed "the fastest woman in Africa" and represented Ghana at the Commonwealth Games.

Father Kevin then made more than 400 appearances across the English Football League during his playing career for clubs including Bristol City, Swindon, Swansea and Exeter.

Now qualified as a personal trainer, Kevin not only helps write training programmes for Amankwaah to use in the gym, but he can also provide the kind of advice ahead of a competition only a professional sportsperson can offer.

"It's definitely helped because he's been through the troubles of sport, so he's very motivating; he's the one I go to if I need any help," Amankwaah said.

"He's really good with helping me focus; he knows what I need before a competition because I get very nervous.

"He knows when to give me a bit of space; he knows what wise words will help me to do the best that I can because he's been through it as well."

Although whether Amankwaah's parents will be able to contain their nerves to watch her compete in Glasgow is another question.

"He [dad] gets really nervous; he tries not to let it on because my mum gets so nervous. Sometimes he even struggles to even film my routines because he's so nervous, but it's just because he wants the best for me," she said.

"They'll also think it will be such a good experience for me, so not too much pressure put on me just because it's my first one; just have to enjoy the moment while I'm young."

title: Kimi K3 found 19 0days in latest Redis 8.8.0 in 1.5hrs
description: RCE PoC for Redis 6.2.22, 7.4.9, 8.
fullText: Redis Authenticated RCE (stream NACK double free, TDigest & TopK module bugs) Non-destructive RCE exploits for Redis 6.2.22, 7.4.9, 8.6.4 via the stream consumer-group shared-NACK double free (a patch bypass of CVE-2026-25243), 8.8.0 via a TDigest heap-overflow in bundled RedisBloom, and 8.8.1 via a TopK wild free (a patch bypass of CVE-2026-25589). Files A_exploit_stock.py — exploit for 6.2.22 (redis:6.2.22 image) P74_exploit.py (+ P74_g2.py) — exploit for 7.4.9 (redis:7.4) P86_exploit.py — exploit for 8.6.4 (redis:8.6) P88W_exploit.py (+ P88W_lib.py, P88W_corrupt.py) — exploit for 8.8.0 (redis:8.8.0, via bundled-module TDigest heap overflow) T88_exploit.py — exploit for 8.8.0 and 8.8.1 (via bundled-module TopK wild free; bypass of the incomplete CVE-2026-25589 fix — works on RedisBloom v8.8.0 and v8.8.2) A_lib.py, G2_arbread.py — shared helpers (must sit next to the exploits) crc64.c/h, crcspeed.c/h — sources for libcrc64.so (Redis CRC64, needed to build valid RESTORE payloads) calibrate.sh — compute binary offsets for non-official builds P74_loop.sh, P86_run.sh — boot-retry wrappers Build (one-time) gcc -shared -fPIC -O2 -o libcrc64.so crc64.c crcspeed.c Requires: Python 3.6+ (no pip packages), gcc. Usage # 6.2.22 (DEBUG enabled by default) python3 A_exploit_stock.py [password] [trigger] # 7.4.9 / 8.6.4 — stock target, NO debug flag needed python3 P74_exploit.py [password] [trigger] python3 P86_exploit.py [password] [trigger] # 8.8.0 — stock target, FRESH container/instance strongly recommended python3 P88W_exploit.py [password] [trigger] # 8.8.0 / 8.8.1 — TopK wild free, stock target, fresh instance recommended python3 T88_exploit.py [password] [trigger] password — omit (or pass "") for no-auth targets trigger — shell command, default writes proof under /data/pwned* Examples: # local lab, 6.2.22 docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis:6.2.22 redis-server --requirepass exploitme python3 A_exploit_stock.py 127.0.0.1 6379 exploitme "id > /data/pwned_stock" # local lab, 7.4.9 docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis:7.4 redis-server --requirepass exploitme python3 P74_exploit.py 127.0.0.1 6379 exploitme "id > /data/pwned74" # local lab, 8.6.4 docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis:8.6 redis-server --requirepass exploitme python3 P86_exploit.py 127.0.0.1 6379 exploitme "id > /data/pwned86" # local lab, 8.8.0 docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis:8.8.0 redis-server --requirepass exploitme python3 P88W_exploit.py 127.0.0.1 6379 exploitme "id > /data/pwned88" python3 T88_exploit.py 127.0.0.1 6379 exploitme "id > /data/pwned_t88" Target requirements 6.2.22: official-image offsets by default. For other builds, override offsets (--str-format-off etc.; see --help), or run ./calibrate.sh /path/to/redis-server [/path/to/libc.so.6] to generate them. Wrong offsets crash the target server (out-of-range reads). 7.4.9 / 8.6.4 / 8.8.0 / 8.8.1: stock official images, no debug flag needed. Commands available to the user: EVAL, RESTORE, XGROUP (8.8.0/8.8.1 also need the bundled RedisBloom module, present by default). 8.8.0/8.8.1 exploits are layout-sensitive: the TDigest corridor and the TopK wild free both depend on jemalloc layout — use a fresh instance with no other clients/commands interleaved, and retry on a grooming miss. Notes Post-run residue (deliberate, inert): a handful of exploit keys that keep double-freed chunks alive; see HARDENING.md for details. The 8.8.0 exploit leaves ~2000 zeroed tdigest structs + a corrupted oracle key — do not FLUSHALL/SAVE the target afterwards. The shared-NACK double free is fixed only in 8.8.0 (PR #15081); the 8.8.0 exploit instead uses a separate, unfixed bundled-module bug. Patch bypasses demonstrated: the shared-NACK variant bypasses the CVE-2026-25243 fix; the TopK wild free bypasses the CVE-2026-25589 fix (partial pointer zeroing — TopK_Destroy still reads all k pointers past the undersized heap in both RedisBloom v8.8.0 and v8.8.2). Reliability: 6.2.22 10/10, 7.4.9 5/5, 8.6.4 25/25, 8.8.0 5/5 validated on fresh containers. Failures abort cleanly; re-run or use wrappers. For authorized testing only.

title: Beyond Static Summarization: Proactive Memory Extraction for LLM Agents
description: Memory management is vital for LLM agents to handle long-term interaction and personalization. Most research focuses on how to organize and use memory summary, but often overlooks the initial memory extraction stage. In this paper, we argue that existing summary-based methods have two major limitations based on the recurrent processing theory. First, summarization is "ahead-of-time", acting as a blind "feed-forward" process that misses important details because it doesn't know future tasks. Second, extraction is usually "one-off", lacking a feedback loop to verify facts, which leads to the accumulation of information loss.

title: Cardiovascular Disease Infographic
Calm technologies that excite me
description: These are some products that I'm excitedly following (or am keen to get) because they're technologies without the intent to dominate.
fullText:
I believe that technology should be an enabler, not a subjugator. The tinkerers that love messing with dials and diving into code should continue to do so! But otherwise, technology should help you do a thing and then stand back as you hold the reins of your human experience. I’ll try to write more about this later on.

For now, though, my main gripe with my tech interactions is how everything has steps. I have to jump through multiple hoops to reach the one thing I want to do in the moment. And even when I am doing a thing, there’s always something else that’s trying to grab my attention — an SMS, a UPI reward unlocked, or a spam call for a credit card.

Even when the device isn’t trying to distract me, I now have an inbuilt Distractor 5000 in my head that impulsively checks all communication channels, just because it can. The drawback of having a rectangle that can do everything is that it can do literally everything, all the time. That can be, safe to say, quite turbulent.

There was a brief Golden Age I experienced in middle school; I would use a Kindle for reading, my mom’s iPod Shuffle for listening to music, and the family computer for any Internet shenanigans. Time felt more dedicated and worry-free back then, even if that’s partly because I was still in school. :P

Replicating that exact setup today would have its own problems, ofcourse — music players would be more useful if they had Bluetooth, and e-readers would be more useful with an independent Internet connection (and without companies locking down the software). But we’ve gotten good at building devices that communicate well with each other, and can do more things when they are offline.

What I dream of is an era of Focused Compute™, where devices target one human function and exist as a thin layer to smoothly do that function. What excites me is that we’re already getting there, from new competing entrants to old products that are being seen in a different light due to paths that software unlocks.

I would like to highlight the ones that really stick out to me this year, and I’m closely tracking to buy. I also hereby give you full permission to gift these to me if you want. I won’t complain. :P

E-ink is very, very cool. It literally moves ink on the screen to change the picture, and doesn’t need any extra power to maintain the state of that ink1. This leads to low power usage and a long battery life, but what I like the most is that these displays can have paper-like textures and still be visible in direct sunlight. They act more like paper that can change content instead of a greyscale display.

The trade-off here is that the ink takes time to move, and changing content is slow. This has been fine for reading books, but not as much for anything else. Fast-moving content was left to normal displays, until Anjan Katta came along with Daylight Computing Co..

This guy worked on a version of Transflective LCDs called LivePaper, which can still be visible under direct sunlight and maintains the speed expected from normal displays (around 60 frames per second).

They’ve showcased it with their first product, the DC-1, which is an Android tablet with a comfy physical build that’s in line with their notion of building a “Calm Computer”. It comes with some homely textured accessories and has a very, very yellow frontlight. I love it.

“Why not go ahead and get it, then?” Well, the DC-1 is still their first iteration at trying out LivePaper displays and I’m observing it from afar. They also don’t currently deliver to India. And even if they did, the DC-1 is currently $729 USD (which is roughly ₹70,200 INR). That’s a lot for an Android tablet with large bezels and a greyscale screen.

But hey, I’m excited for a future where reading blogs and documents doesn’t need me to look at some really bright colourful pixels. Imagine reading out in the Sun with this thing!

It was around last year when my YouTube algorithm slowly started moving towards videos of iPods being restored; sometimes receiving storage or battery upgrades. That slowly turned into adding USB-C, then having a custom exterior, and then rooting the operating system to be completely different.

As smartphones arrived, music players slowly reduced to either really simple ones with basic playback controls, or really sophisticated ones for audiophiles (by companies like Fiio). The mainstream was left to smartphones, and later on to streaming services like Spotify.

I’d been fine with streaming music on the daily, until a few things merged into a giant snowball of reasons to move away from the green Wi-Fi symbol:

The final blow was when I heard about how the CEO of Spotify invested a large sum of money into military drones and other defense technologies. Money that he received from subscriptions like the Family Plan I pay for. People sometimes tell me that everything indirectly contributes to evil and we can’t really do much about it, but I felt too close to evil in this case. So I decided to start shifting.

The first iteration of migrating was using this app called Classipod, which simulates the iPod interface on your phone. I downloaded FLAC versions of my favourite albums and just started listening.

The experience was much calmer than before, since my options were limited and I chose the albums wisely. Streaming had turned listening to music into a constant background activity; sound that kept droning on without distinction between songs or lyrics. The intentionality of “turning on the iPod” and choosing an album to queue was more mechanical and felt more fruitful, which stuck with me.

The current scene for purchasing a modded iPod in India is still, ofcourse, quite low. The two places I found (neither of which I’ve actually purchased from, FYI) were The Revolver Club (which had limited options) and RetroPodz (which had AI-generated images but is run by one dude as a hobby). I’m still conflicted about the product that will fulfil this need of mine2, but a music player is definitely on the list.

Smartphones are a pain, aren’t they?

We use the same portal for accessing both our loved ones and some of the worst events currently going on in the world. It’s the harbinger of both good news and the bad — from breakup texts to salary updates, from rejection emails to messages from old friends. This is my completely unbiased opinion. :)

None of that relates to this specific product, though.

We got the first smartphone in the family at the start of the 2010’s. Before that, it was all squat button phones from Nokia and Motorola. I didn’t particularly care for the form factor, all I remember is playing Bounce Tales on my Nannoo’s Nokia E63.

What I realize looking back though is that a lot of the doom content consumption today is dependent on the vertical form factor. First it was just short-form video, but platforms like Instagram have also made 4:3 photographs their new default (a choice that has faced much criticism from artists).

I feel like a lot of that could be fixed (or rather, I could steer myself away from doomscrolling) by just not having a screen that shows this content properly. This is where I’d like to introduce my solution, the Clicks Communicator.

Worked on by Michael Fischer (Mr. Mobile)‘s company Clicks, the Communicator is meant to be a secondary Blackberry-like device meant primarily for communication during working hours. Its main pitch is “focus and simplicity in communication”, which I’m reading as “an attempt to best distract you from distractions”.

The device has a physical keyboard as well as a square touchsreen. The keyboard’s spacebar also functions as a fingerprint sensor, and there’s a special key you can use to create any shortcuts you want. It’s lighter than the average smartphone and has a “signal light” on the side which is similar to the notification LED’s you’d get on older smartphones.

I love this as my concept for minimalism because it doesn’t compromise on all the extensibility we’ve gained from modern technologies. I shouldn’t have to move away from the gains of WhatsApp or a dedicated maps application just to take back my time and focus. This also comes at a good time where the outer screens of modern flip phones have the same screen aspect ratio as the Communicator, meaning that developers have more incentive to fit their apps to such smaller screens.

The current drawbacks are led by the fact that this phone doesn’t currently exist; even the one showed above is a dummy for media coverage. The company has started taking reservations for the phone, but it will still be another year before they can start shipping them out by current progress speeds. With some leeway to check other people’s reviews after that, there’s definitely some time before I can get something like this. And oh, they don’t ship to India right now.

Returning to my love for e-ink, TRMNL is an ecosystem where you can set up multiple e-ink displays as periodically updating dashboards for whatever you want. This can either be a new display/setup that you buy from them directly, or you can give old e-readers a new life by connecting them to TRMNL.

In technical terms, TRMNL works by having a central web server (either self-hosted or provided by trmnl.com), which sends images to the e-ink device every 30 seconds. The receiver then accepts the image and updates the data on its display.

This lets the device maintain its low-power and passive nature, with the addition of only one-way communication so the device does not send data back to the server.

I really like this idea of data calmly updating in the background, like a photo frame I can turn to and see the information I need at a glance. The current ideas to use it are,

The best part is that the entire ecosystem can be self-hosted — an old Kindle can become a receiver, and you can host your own web server on something like a Raspberry Pi. There’s also a large library of integrations with the ability to create your own plugins for any platform you want. I really respect the determination of the developers to keep it all open-source.

A lot of my thoughts worthy of pursuing pass by like blips. I have to quickly catch them off the train of thought and store them somewhere to revisit later.

I’ve tried doing this with sending WhatsApp messages to myself or using my phone’s audio recorder, but they both have the same problem of requiring too many steps between thinking and recording.

I want it to be as simple as maybe holding a button that starts recording my voice and saves it on release — which does exist on some phones, but is being portrayed as an AI feature and is most likely sending that data somewhere else.

The last time you probably saw one was in the hands of a journalist, interviewer, or a documentary anchor. They’re meant for recording conversations or solo “in-the-moment” experiences that can later be accumulated and looked back upon.

There’s a large range for these products thanks to their professional purpose and demand. I’ve not researched them that well yet, but it is my assumption that these things just store audio files which you can then connect and import over. That’s only half the equation for me.

My goal with a dictaphone would be to automatically receive the files at, say, the end of the day and transcribe them so I can search through them or summarize the ideas. I can see it being useful for writing weeknotes, or otherwise compiling thoughts I’ve had about certain projects over time. They’re tiny too (relative to smartphones), so it would a small addition to my EDC.

P.S.: There’s this field recorder from Teenage Engineering called the TP-7. It plays music, looks beautiful, can scrub through tracks by rotating the disc, and can record notes that can be transcribed through a companion mobile app. It’s a piece of art that records things as a side hustle.

The only problem, again, is that the thing $1,499 USD (roughly ₹1,44,000 INR). What in the world.

I think we’ve caught on to the fact that gaining more information and speed through our tools doesn’t particularly mean that we get to relax more; in most cases, it just means that our deadlines get shorter. A busy day can leave us catching our breath afterwards, and the rest we take needs to keep technology at an arm’s length, as much as it can.

The desire for calmer compute has grown past the stage of indie projects and tiny scales. We deserve rest, and a space where our thoughts can be mulled over without being bombarded with information. Let’s look for technologies that give us that space.

Any compute or frontlight in front of the display still uses power, ofcourse. But the display itself can retain images without power. ↩

Another product that I’m following is the Fiio Snowsky Disc. ↩

title: Google launches selfie video sign-in
description: Google introduced selfie video sign-in, a new way to recover a Google Account. But what does this mean for your biometric data privacy?
fullText:
It’s pitched as an easier way back into your account when you’re locked out. Before you record your face for Google (or whether you should), three questions are worth asking: where does the video live, how is it protected, and what else might Google do with it?

On 23 July 2026, Google introduced selfie video sign-in, a new way to recover a Google Account. Instead of a password or a code, you record a short video of your face, and Google checks it against a selfie you enrolled earlier to let you back in.

It also fits a pattern worth noticing. First Google began testing a reCAPTCHA that asks you to wave your hand at the camera; now it wants a short video of your face to sign you in. Each step asks for a little more of your biometric data to prove you’re human.

It’s easy to see the appeal. Passwords get forgotten and phones get lost. But this replaces a thing you know for a copy of your face sitting on Google’s servers, and a face is not a password you can change if it ever leaks. So the details of where it goes, and who gets to use it, matter more than usual.

Join the MEGA privacy revolution. Create a free MEGA account and get 20 GB of storage with zero-knowledge encryption.

Setup asks you to look into your camera and complete a few guided head movements, capturing your face from several angles. If you later get locked out, whether from a forgotten password, a lost or stolen phone, or a sign-in on a borrowed device, you record a fresh selfie and Google compares it to the enrolled one to confirm it’s really you.

To stop people fooling it with a photo or a deepfake, Google matches the live video against your saved selfie, asks you to perform simple movements to prove you’re a real, live person, and runs its usual checks for suspicious sign-ins. As a security upgrade over a recycled password, it looks like a genuine step forward. The privacy questions sit underneath it.

This isn’t stored only on your phone. For recovery to work when your device is lost or you’re on someone else’s, the enrolled selfie has to live on Google’s side, associated with your Google Account.

The (almost) reassuring part is that Google says the selfie is recorded and stored only with your consent, and that you can delete it at any time from your account settings. So you’re not locked into keeping it. But while it’s enrolled, a biometric record of your face is being held by Google, not by you.

Google says the selfie video is encrypted at rest, meaning it’s protected while it’s sitting in storage and not in use. That’s good practice, and it guards against the file being lifted straight out of a database.

It’s worth being clear about what “encrypted at rest” does and doesn’t mean, because the phrase does a lot of quiet reassuring. Yes, it protects the stored file from outside theft. But, it does not mean Google can’t see your face. Google holds the keys and has to be able to process your selfie to match it during recovery, so this is company-managed encryption, not end-to-end or zero-knowledge encryption where the provider has no access to your content at all. Your face is protected from others. It isn’t hidden from Google.

This is the question worth slowing down on, and the honest answer is: by default, no, but there’s a door.

Google says the selfie is used solely for sign-in unless you choose to share it for other purposes. The catch is in that second clause. During or after setup, you can opt in to let Google use the video and related data to improve its facial recognition, age estimation, and other identity-verification technologies. In plain terms, there’s a setting that turns your recovery selfie into training data for Google’s biometric models.

It’s opt-in, not automatic, and that’s to Google’s credit. But two things are true at once. A default-off toggle still normalises the idea of handing your face to a company to train its systems. And “used solely for sign-in” is a policy and a settings state, both of which can be changed, misread, or quietly re-defaulted in a future update. The protection holds as long as you decline the option and Google keeps the arrangement it describes today.

So, are we sure Google won’t use your face for its own purposes? You can be reasonably sure it won’t if you don’t opt in. You cannot be sure the option to will always stay off by default, or that the boundary won’t move.

For a lot of people, selfie recovery will be safer than the password sticky-note or the recycled login it replaces, and that’s a real benefit. This isn’t a scandal, per se. It’s a trade, and it’s worth making with your eyes open.

There’s also a way to need recovery far less often. Most lockouts come down to a forgotten password or the same one reused everywhere. A password manager removes that weak point: if every login is unique and stored somewhere you can actually get to it, the moment you’d reach for a face scan rarely arrives. MEGA Pass creates and stores a unique password for every account, with built-in two-factor authentication, so getting locked out becomes the rare exception rather than a routine event.

If you do enrol, the practical moves are simple: the classic think before you click, make sure you‘re not opting in to anything, check that setting again after major account updates, and remember you can delete the enrolled selfie whenever you like. And weigh, as you would with any biometric feature, whether the convenience is worth handing a copy of your face to a company at all. In this case, the company is Google, the biggest out there known to monetise user data.

It’s also a reminder that “encrypted” isn’t one thing. Encryption that a company can reverse protects you from outsiders but not from the company. Encryption where the provider holds no keys protects you from both. That’s the standard MEGA is built on for your files in MEGA Cloud, and it’s a useful yardstick to hold up against any service asking to store something as permanent as your face.

Try zero-knowledge encryption for yourself. Create a free MEGA account and get 20 GB of storage with zero-knowledge encryption.

On Google’s servers, linked to your Google Account, so it can be used for recovery even if your device is lost. Google says it’s stored only with your consent and can be deleted at any time from your account settings.

Google says it’s encrypted at rest, which protects the stored file from theft. It is not end-to-end or zero-knowledge encryption, so Google can still access and process your face to perform the sign-in match.

Not by default. Google says the selfie is used only for sign-in unless you opt in to let it improve facial recognition, age estimation, and other identity-verification technology. That option is a choice you can decline.

Yes. Google says you can remove it at any time from your Google Account settings.

For account recovery, it can be, because it’s harder to phish or guess than a password and includes liveness checks against deepfakes. The trade-off is that it stores a biometric record of your face, which, unlike a password, you can’t change if it’s ever exposed.

title: Chess Legend Vishy Anand Becomes Interim FIDE President
description: NEW DELHI: International Chess Federation (FIDE) President Arkady Dvorkovich has stepped aside from his role after being included in the European Union's latest sanctions list linked to Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine. The decision means FIDE Deputy President and former world champion Viswanathan Anand will serve as the interim president until the sanctions are lifted or overturned.
fullText:
Arkady Dvorkovich (on left) and Viswanathan Anand NEW DELHI: International Chess Federation (FIDE) President Arkady Dvorkovich has stepped aside from his role after being included in the European Union's latest sanctions list linked to Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine. The decision means FIDE Deputy President and former world champion Viswanathan Anand will serve as the interim president until the sanctions are lifted or overturned.In a statement released by FIDE, Dvorkovich described the sanctions as unjust and confirmed that he would challenge them through legal channels. However, he said his decision to temporarily suspend himself was taken to ensure the smooth functioning of the global governing body. Watch Harika Dronavalli Exclusive: Inside Grenke #chess win, viral no-handshake incident, and more"Today, the EU decision to include my name in the 21st sanctions list was published. I want to state unequivocally that I consider this decision unlawful and unfair, and this decision will be immediately challenged by all possible means. I am confident that justice will prevail," FIDE's statement reads.He added that remaining in office while under sanctions could affect FIDE's day-to-day operations."However, given that until a formal court decision is rendered and/or such decision is cancelled or amended, these sanctions could hinder the stable functioning of FIDE, I have decided to voluntarily suspend the exercise of my powers and duties as FIDE President with immediate effect and for as long as I remain subject to sanctions under the EU and Swiss laws and regulations."Under Article 19.2 of the FIDE Charter, Anand will now take over as Interim President.The FIDE Council formally accepted Dvorkovich's decision and confirmed that he would not exercise any presidential powers or control over the federation or its assets while the sanctions remain in force.The FIDE presidential election at the Chess Olympiad in Samarkand will go ahead as scheduled.AICF welcomes Anand's new roleThe move was welcomed by All India Chess Federation (AICF) President Nitin Narang, who expressed confidence in Anand's leadership."As FIDE enters an interim phase of leadership, I extend my best wishes to Deputy President Viswanathan Anand as he assumes the responsibilities of Interim President," he wrote on X."Vishy sir has earned the respect of the global chess community through his integrity, wisdom and lifelong service to our sport. I am confident that under his stewardship, FIDE will continue to uphold stability, good governance and its commitment to players and federations across the world."The EU's 21st sanctions package targets 170 entities and 48 individuals with measures including possible asset freezes and travel bans. Despite the leadership change, FIDE said its activities, tournaments and social programmes will continue without interruption.India's Chess Olympiad squads announcedMeanwhile, India has also announced its squads for the FIDE Chess Olympiad in Samarkand, Uzbekistan, to be held from September 17 to 27.Defending champions India will be led by reigning world champion D Gukesh, alongside R Praggnanandhaa, Arjun Erigaisi, Nihal Sarin and Vidit Gujrathi in the open section, while Koneru Humpy headlines the women's team with R Vaishali, Divya Deshmukh, Vantika Agrawal and Savitha Shri B.

title: Jeffrey Donaldson stripped of knighthood by King
description: The former DUP leader was convicted in June of 18 counts of child sex abuse.
fullText:
Former Democratic Unionist Party (DUP) leader Jeffrey Donaldson has been stripped of his knighthood by the King.

Donaldson, who was an MP from 1997 until 2024, was convicted in June of 18 counts of sex abuse including one count of rape, which he committed against two women when they were children.

In the aftermath of his conviction he asked to be removed from the Privy Council and for his knighthood to be forfeited.

On Thursday a notice was published in The Gazette - one of the UK's official journals of record - stating that Charles III had directed the cancellation of Donaldson's knighthood.

The notice, published by the Central Chancery of the Orders of Knighthood stated: "THE KING has directed the cancellation and annulment of the knighthood conferred upon Jeffrey Mark Donaldson on the 11 June 2016 as a Knight Bachelor."

Donaldson was awarded the honour in the Queen's Birthday Honours list in 2016.

He was removed from the Privy Council, which advises the monarch, earlier in July.

The forfeiture of his knighthood means his wife Eleanor Donaldson loses the courtesy title of Lady Donaldson.

She had faced a trial of the facts on mental health grounds, meaning the jury was asked to find out if she did the acts or did not do the acts of which she was accused.

They found that she had done the acts in relation to all five charges – including four counts of aiding and abetting her husband's offending.

The DUP has commissioned an independent review into Donaldson's time in the party.

He was previously a member of the Ulster Unionist Party, which has said it will appoint an independent, external organisation to establish a process for people to confidentially share information about Jeffrey Donaldson during his time in the party.

Donaldson was convicted unanimously on all counts by a jury of seven men and five women at Newry Crown Court.

He was told by the judge that the sentence would be a lengthy one.

It has since been confirmed he is to appeal his convictions.

His legal team lodged the paperwork with the Court of Appeal in Belfast on 17 July.

His attempt to have his convictions overturned is unlikely to play out before he is sentenced.

The process will involve a hearing at which leave to appeal would either be granted or denied - it is speculated this could happen sometime in September.

Should leave be granted, a full hearing would then follow at a later stage.

An honour such as a knighthood can be removed on the advice of a body known as the Forfeiture Committee if the holder is deemed to have brought the honours system into disrepute.

Recommendations to remove an honour are considered byt the committee and if it agrees to remove an honour, that advice is passed onto the King via the prime minister.

If the King gives his approval a notice is then normally placed in the Gazette.

The holder of the honour is then asked to return their insignia to Buckingham Palace and cannot use the title or post-nominal letters any more.

Donaldson to give up knighthood and seat on Privy Council

title: US health officials are investigating a new outbreak of a diarrhea-causing parasite
description: The Food and Drug Administration has not yet identified which products are linked to the illnesses.  “This is an active investigation, and officials are working together to determine the specific caus...
fullText:
Federal health officials are investigating a new outbreak involving the diarrhea-causing parasite cyclospora.

The outbreak included 72 people as of Wednesday.

The Food and Drug Administration has not yet identified which products are linked to the illnesses. Officials also have not said where the cases are located.

"This is an active investigation, and officials are working together to determine the specific cause of illnesses in this cluster," the Department of Health and Human Services said in an email Thursday.

Federal officials earlier this week said they remained focused on lettuce from Taylor Farms as the source of a separate, multistate outbreak. The company last week announced it was recalling iceberg lettuce grown in central Mexico.

Cyclospora is a microscopic parasite that infects food that has come into contact with human feces, most commonly when produce is irrigated or washed with contaminated water. When ingested, the parasite causes intestinal illness marked by "frequent and sometimes explosive bowel movements," according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

Federal and state health authorities have been investigating separate clusters of cyclospora illnesses, including the one traced to Taylor Farms.

Food safety tips include washing hands with soap and water after using the bathroom and before handling food. Experts say cooking can kill the parasite and advise cooking produce to a temperature of at least 158 degrees Fahrenheit (70 degrees Celsius.)
