title: Lyles, Richardson ease into 100m semis at US athletics championships
description: Olympic gold medallist Noah Lyles and Sha'Carri Richardson eased into the 100m semi-finals at the US athletics championships on Thursday.Lyles, who captured 100m gold at the Paris Olympics in 2024, won his heat in 9.97sec to book his semi-final berth with the second-quickest time of the day behind the 9.96 posted by former world champion Christian Coleman.
fullText:
Olympic gold medallist Noah Lyles and Sha'Carri Richardson eased into the 100m semi-finals at the US athletics championships on Thursday.

Lyles, who captured 100m gold at the Paris Olympics in 2024, won his heat in 9.97sec to book his semi-final berth with the second-quickest time of the day behind the 9.96 posted by former world champion Christian Coleman.

Richardson, the 2023 world champion and 2024 Olympic silver medallist, topped the women's heats in 10.90sec.

Richardson will be a heavy favorite for the title on Friday, with Melissa Jefferson-Wooden -- a treble gold medallist at last year's world championships -- opting to race the 200m only this week.

Lyles called his championships opener a good warm-up "just to get the engine firing."

But he predicted a "fast final" on Friday on the Icahn Stadium track, where Usain Bolt set a 100m world record of 9.72sec in 2008 -- a mark the Jamaican eventually lowered to the current 9.58.

Coleman, the 2019 world champion and the 60m indoor world record-holder, was also pleased with his "really smooth" start to the meeting.

"I was able to break 10 (seconds) without really hitting the gas like I know I can, so I feel good about it," said Coleman, who topped the times despite racing into a slight headwind of 0.3m/sec.

Kenny Bednarek, a two-time Olympic silver medallist at 200m, won his 100m heat to advance.

Among a handful of finals on the opening day of competition, Woody Kincaid surged late to win the men's 10,000m in 30min 13.66sec ahead of 2024 Olympic bronze medallist Grant Fisher, who clocked 30:14.46.

Sydney Vaught won the women's 10,000m in 33:36.98.

Isaac Grimes claimed his second straight men's long jump title, clearing 8.18m on his final attempt. Malcolm Clemons was second with a leap of 8.07m.

title: Real Madrid unveil dark green away kit for the 2026-27 season
description: Real Madrid's new dark green away strip blends modern adidas design with a nod to the club's past
fullText:
Real Madrid have officially revealed their new away kit for the 2026-27 campaign, completing the club’s matchday collection after previously launching the home strip ahead of the new season.

The new adidas design features a dark green base with off-white accents, reviving a color combination that has appeared in the club’s history while giving it a modern look. The jersey includes a subtle geometric pattern incorporating Real Madrid’s initials, an off-white trimmed collar, and the adidas Trefoil logo, blending the brand’s heritage with its current football aesthetic.

These are awesome I better not catch anyone complaining about them. pic.twitter.com/Vb0g8oioH6

— Managing Madrid (@managingmadrid) July 23, 2026

According to the club, the shirt is built with adidas’ latest Climacool+ technology, designed to improve ventilation and moisture management to help players stay cool during matches.

The launch campaign is fronted by Kylian Mbappé, Jude Bellingham, and Vinícius Júnior, with additional promotional images featuring Federico Valverde, Arda Güler, Aurélien Tchouaméni, Brahim Díaz, Antonio Rüdiger, Athenea del Castillo, and Linda Caicedo.

While green is not traditionally associated with Real Madrid, it has appeared on a handful of occasions throughout the club’s history. The first came in 1965 during a tour of South America, when Madrid wore borrowed green shirts in a friendly against River Plate in Caracas after the two teams’ regular kits clashed. The match finished in a 1-1 draw. More recently, adidas introduced a green third kit for the 2012-13 season, making this the first time in over a decade that the color returns to the club’s official match wardrobe.

Real Madrid also unveiled the goalkeeper’s away kit alongside the outfield strip. The goalkeeper jersey features a light blue design with darker blue shading along the sleeves and sides, complemented by fluorescent yellow adidas stripes. It joins the previously released black goalkeeper kit for the upcoming campaign.

The new away kit is available now through Real Madrid’s official stores, adidas retailers, and adidas’ online store. Adult replica jerseys begin at €100.

title: Dillon Dingler, James Outman deliver in clutch in Tigers' comeback win
description: Dillon Dingler delivered a two-out RBI and James Outman hit a two-out, three-run homer in Thursday's 4-3 win.
fullText:
Detroit — Dillon Dingler, as he has all season, delivered another clutch, two-out RBI.

This one sent the Tigers to a 4-3 win over the Kansas City Royals in front of 35,548 at Comerica Park Thursday night.

With Hao-Yu Lee at second base, Dingler lined a Steven Cruz pitch into the left-field corner to break the tie. It was his 35th two-out RBI, and his team-high 66th, this season.

With Kenley Jansen unavailable after pitching in three of the last four games, with Tyler Holton already collecting five outs in the game, and with the Royals having two lefties coming up, lefty Drew Sommers started the ninth. But after striking out Carter Jensen, he walked Lane Thomas and gave up a single to Vinnie Pasquantino.

Manager AJ Hinch summoned Kyle Finnegan. He promptly induced a 4-6-3 double play from Salvador Perez to end the game.

The Tigers (49-54) are now 12-5 in July and 27-16 since June 1.

They were down 2-0 after six innings. But like on Wednesday, they had a clutch swing looming.

After Lee delivered a clutch three-run home run to beat the Cubs in the finale in Chicago, it was light-hitting James Outman’s turn to play unlikely hero Thursday.

Against former Tiger John Schreiber, Outman, who came in hitting .155, hit a two-out, three-run home run in the bottom of the seventh that gave the Tigers a short-lived 3-2 lead.

Outman drove a center-cut, 2-2 fastball into the tiny shrubs just over the wall in right field for his fourth homer since joining the Tigers.

that ball is OUT MAN ‼️ pic.twitter.com/vAt4fqck3S

— Detroit Tigers (@tigers) July 24, 2026

That lead lasted three batters. Against reliever Drew Anderson, Michael Massey singled, stole second, went to third on a wild pitch and scored on a single by Josh Rojas.

Schreiber was the second former Tiger in the game for the Royals.

It wasn’t too awfully long ago (2017) that Randy Dobnak was pitching in an independent league in Utica. He reached the big leagues in 2019 and beat the Tigers at Comerica Park in his fifth start that season.

Last year, he made eight starts for the Triple-A Toledo Mud Hens but never earned a call-up from the Tigers, who rolled the dice in early September with veterans Chris Paddack and Charlie Morton.

Dobnak showed them what they might’ve been missing Thursday night.

Short-arming a clever mix of changeups, sinkers and sweepers, he stifled the Tigers’ hitters for five innings. They managed just four hits and the only time they threatened was in the first. With runners at the corners and two outs, Dobnak got Dingler to rollover to shortstop.

For Tigers starter Troy Melton, it was a struggle from the start.

He needed 28 pitches to get through the first inning, walking two batters around a single by Pasquantino. But with the bases loaded and one out, he struck out Michael Massey and Isaac Collins to end the threat.

His third strike to Collins bounced away from Dingler. Dingler pounced on it and Melton alertly covered the plate, just barely getting his foot on the plate before Carter Jensen slid home.

Melton ended up walking four hitters and stranded runners in scoring position in the second and fifth innings.

It caught up with him in the sixth. He gave up a single to Massey then left a slider over the middle of the plate to Collins, who knocked it into the right-field seats.

This article originally appeared on The Detroit News: Dillon Dingler, James Outman deliver in clutch in Tigers' comeback win

title: Coypa is a fun way to manage your clipboard
description: A fun way to manage your clipboard. Contribute to willmeyers/coypa development by creating an account on GitHub.
fullText:
coypa is a fun way to manage your clipboard.

 Copies stack and follow your cursor. Hold paste, they fan out
 into a wheel. Pick one, and it pastes!

coypa has to notice the paste key being held anywhere, which macOS only
permits through a keyboard event tap:

System Settings ▸ Privacy & Security ▸ Accessibility → add coypa.app

Grant it to the app bundle, not your terminal — macOS ties this permission
to an app's code-signing identity, so running the bare binary attaches the
grant to whatever launched it.

Without permission coypa still collects copies; only the hold-to-wheel trigger
goes quiet, and ⌘V behaves exactly as normal.

title: Grocery Shopping in the Age of Explosive Diarrhea
description: The cyclospora outbreak lays bare our underfunded and overwhelmed public-health system.
fullText:
Within an hour of receiving my friend’s text, I had forty-eight cyclosporiasis-related tabs open on my laptop. Cyclospora, I learned, is a protozoan parasite found in produce that has been tainted with human feces—perhaps because heavy rains flooded the porta-potties on the farm where the produce was grown, or because it was irrigated or washed with contaminated water. The cyclospora parasite travels inside an oocyst, a sturdy pod that can find purchase in the nooks and crannies of berries or cling to the ridges and folds of certain greens—and it will die there only if cooked. Once these oocysts reach the human digestive tract, the outer coating dissolves and the parasite unleashes a Visigoth invasion of the small intestine, causing nausea, cramping, and what has been described as “watery diarrhea,” “relentless diarrhea,” and, most vividly, “explosive diarrhea.”

“It’s uncontrollable and it’s very liquid,” Joseph Eisenberg, an epidemiologist at the University of Michigan, told me. “If you’ve seen pictures from cholera wards, where the patients are on gurneys, and the gurney has a hole where your butt goes, and there’s a bucket underneath the hole—it’s very similar.” Cyclosporiasis can be treated with antibiotics and is usually not life-threatening, but the miserable symptoms may persist for a month or more.

Tracking the origins of a food-borne illness traditionally demands painstaking lab work and exhaustive interviews with patients, who may struggle to recall what and where they ate weeks after the fact. Cyclospora is an especially elusive offender because, unlike such bacteria as E. coli or salmonella, it can’t be cultured in a lab. “The problem with cyclospora is that it does not grow outside of the human body, and first it needs a host,” Felicia Wu, who is a professor in the Department of Food Science and Nutrition at Michigan State University, told me. “You have to wait until somebody gets sick and then test the fecal sample, and say, ‘Yup, we see it.’ ”

title: Advancing Mathematics Research with AI-Driven Formal Proof Search
description: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly excel at mathematical reasoning, but their unreliability limits their utility in mathematics research. A mitigation is using LLMs to generate formal proofs in languages like Lean. We perform the first large-scale evaluation of this method's ability to solve open problems. Our most capable agent autonomously resolved 9 of 353 open Erdős problems at the per-problem cost of a few hundred dollars, proved 44/492 OEIS conjectures, and is being deployed in combinatorics, optimization, graph theory, algebraic geometry, and quantum optics research. A basic agent alternating LLM-based generation with Lean-based verification replicated the Erdős successes but proved costlier on the hardest problems.

title: Broadscale Deep Learning for Maya Settlement Detection in G-LiHT Lidar
description: Examining Lidar data is an efficient way to detect ancient Maya features across the Yucatan Peninsula. Automated object detection powered by deep learning leverages Maya archaeologists’ specialist knowledge in detecting the presence of ancient Maya settlements. By using a broadscale approach in its training, our new efficient multi-regional model Q2000 achieves comparable performance across a significantly broader and more diverse geographic region. This study addresses the current limitation of small-scale, area-specific models to generalize characteristics and properly detect a diverse range of target objects over a large area. This study introduces the foundational development of a broadscale, multi-region convolutional neural network (CNN) object detection model utilizing Lidar data across a significantly larger extent of the Maya area (approximately 35,584 km2).

title: Computational Identifiability
description: Identification conditions describe the computability of a target query or parameter of interest as a function of the type and amount of information available. In causal identification, this information is often expressed in the form of a causal graph, and data are observed or collected for some subset of variables in the graph. Target queries may be for a single effect alone or for a class of effects in a given model. The derivation of an identification algorithm then defines mathematically the process by which the desired causal effect(s) can be uniquely determined, theoretically, in expectation. Identifiability in expectation, or 'theoretical identifiability,' generally assumes asymptotic properties, infinite data, or other mathematically idealized conditions.

title: House and Senate split on votes to rebuke Trump on Iran war
description: House lawmakers voted to cease the conflict, while the Senate stalled a similar effort just hours later.
fullText:
Congress delivered President Donald Trump a mixed message Thursday on his handling of the war in Iran, just as hostilities flare in the Middle East.

The two chambers split on whether to halt the war in a pair of hotly contested ballots. House Democrats — aided by a handful of Republicans — succeeded for a second time in rebuking Trump over the military campaign. But the Senate GOP rejected a similar effort just hours later, as Democrats couldn't muster the needed support to advance another anti-war measure.

The dueling votes highlight the challenges lawmakers face in reining in Trump and stopping the nearly five-month conflict.

The House vote is another sign that lawmakers are increasingly unhappy with the conflict, as peace talks founder and the two sides sharply escalated hostilities in recent weeks — resulting in the deaths of four U.S. military personnel in the Middle East over the weekend. It also comes as Republicans attempt to hold onto their narrow majority in the House in the November elections.

But the Senate's lack of action underscores the myriad hurdles facing Democrats, and some Republicans, as they weigh an endgame for the conflict. Both the House and Senate cleared legislation to end the war in June. Trump is certain to ignore those votes and is likely to do so again.

The resolutions are legally untested and largely symbolic. The House measure would not go to Trump to sign into law, and the president would almost certainly veto any binding legislation that restricts his authority.

Four Republicans broke ranks to help approve a Democratic-led war powers resolution in a 214-208 vote. It was the second time the House has narrowly rejected the renewed war. Four Republicans who previously voted to end the war did so Thursday: Reps. Tom Barrett of Michigan, Warren Davidson of Ohio, Brian Fitzpatrick of Pennsylvania and Thomas Massie of Kentucky.

The Senate followed by rejecting an effort to advance its own anti-war resolution, offered by Sen. Chris Van Hollen (D-Md.), in a 47-49 vote. Just one Republican, Sen. Susan Collins on Maine, backed the effort, while Democratic Sen. John Fetterman of Pennsylvania opposed it. Two Republicans who previously supported legislation to end the war, Sens. Rand Paul of Kentucky and Lisa Murkowski of Alaska, did not vote.

House Democrats touted the victory, which again demonstrated the minority party can pick off enough Republicans to hand Trump an embarrassing loss. Democrats have relied on the war powers measures — one of the few tools at their disposal — to force Republicans on the record over the unpopular conflict, as the GOP risks being blamed for high gas prices just months ahead of midterm elections.

"What this reflects is that we will continue to put pressure on this president to stand up and to stop this war," said Rep. Pramila Jayapal (D-Wash.), the sponsor of the resolution. "Working in a bipartisan way, we want to make sure that all the Republicans stand up for the Constitution, for their conscience, and for their constituents."

The war, launched in February by the U.S. and Israel, is now in its fifth month and shows no signs of ending soon. A shaky ceasefire first declared in April collapsed earlier this month, as the U.S. and Iran traded attacks and Trump reimposed a naval blockade of Iran's ports.

The White House had argued that the ceasefire stopped a 60-day deadline to end the conflict without congressional buy-in under the War Powers Resolution. The president then notified lawmakers last week that the nation was once again at war with Tehran, in a bid to reset the 60-day clock. Critics contend that the administration's justification is a misreading of the war powers law, noting that the U.S. has engaged in hostilities — namely a naval blockade — even during the ceasefire.

Despite the handful of GOP defections, most Republicans stuck with Trump.

"There were a small number of Republicans that joined us to pass this bill, but it is important to note that the vast majority of Republicans did not follow their consciences and did not listen to their constituents, and voted instead just to keep on spending billions more dollars on this war," said Rep. Greg Casar (D-Texas), who chairs the Congressional Progressive Caucus.

House Foreign Affairs Chair Brian Mast (R-Fla.) said the vote shows "that you have a number of members that have zero recognition" for the sacrifice of U.S. troops and the attacks by Iran in the region.

Leo Shane contributed to this report.

title: FDA says new cyclospora outbreak linked to unspecified product
description: Federal health officials said they've identified a new outbreak of the cyclospora parasite, but the source hasn't been specified.
fullText:
Federal health officials have identified a new cyclospora outbreak stemming from an unspecified product, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration said Thursday. The FDA said the source of the outbreak was "not yet identified."

A spokesperson for the agency told CBS News that the FDA had initiated a traceback, which is when investigators follow the path of a potentially contaminated food product through the supply chain in an effort to identify the origins of a foodborne illness outbreak.

"This is an active investigation," the spokesperson said. "We will provide updates as they become available."

This is the latest among several investigations the FDA has opened into cyclospora outbreaks around the country, including one linked to iceberg lettuce from Taylor Farms that's been blamed for thousands of illnesses in states including Michigan, Ohio, Indiana and Kentucky. That investigation led Taylor Farms to voluntarily recall all of its iceberg lettuce sourced from central Mexico.

A separate outbreak has resulted in at least 10 cases, the FDA said, while yet another outbreak has ended but remains under investigation.

Cyclospora is a microscopic parasite that typically infects people through contaminated food or water. It causes a gastrointestinal illness called cyclosporiasis, which can result in symptoms like severe and sometimes "explosive" diarrhea.

Cooking can kill the parasite, but health officials say just washing fruits and vegetables is not enough.

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said it has received reports of 1,645 confirmed cases of cyclosporiasis since May 1, including 141 patients who were hospitalized. The CDC said it is aware of more than 5,100 cases that require further analysis to confirm.

However, state health departments in affected areas have tallied much higher numbers. Michigan health officials said Thursday that there are actually 7,664 confirmed cases of cyclosporiasis in the state, CBS News Detroit reported.

"Because cyclosporiasis is often underdiagnosed and underreported, the true number of illnesses is likely higher than what has been reported," the CDC noted.

title: Trump dismissed affordability as a word ‘made up by the Democrats’ and promised oil would come ‘tumbling down’—then it hit $100 a barrel
description: At his rally, Trump admitted, “You may be paying a little bit more,” and pleaded for more time, while saying the Iranians “break every deal they make.”
fullText:
President Trump told a cheering crowd at Wheeler High School in Marietta, Ga., on July 22 that "affordability" was a term invented by his political opponents, recounting the exact moment he says he first heard it: at his very first press conference after taking office, when a reporter pressed him on rising egg prices.

"They used the word—first time I heard it. It was a word, you know, made up by the Democrats: affordability," Trump said, describing his response at the time as, "I've been here one day. Don't worry. I'll take care of it." He blamed his predecessor Joe Biden and the "radical left Democrats" for what he called "the worst inflation in 48 years," citing Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent as his source for the figure. In fact, the 2022 inflation surge was the worst in roughly 40 years, with Yale economist Stephen Roach noting that Trump often repeats the claim, one of his "biggest economic lies."

Trump's rally in Marietta was a case study in what that reframing costs him. The president's economic narrative—eggs down, oil coming down, stocks up, 401(k)s flush—is accurate in pieces and misleading in sum. The stock market has hit 73 all-time highs since the election while consumer confidence is near historic lows, and both things are true because the market rally is riding on a rickety bet on AI, with tech megacaps' earnings not translating into lower grocery bills or affordable mortgages. The president spent Wednesday night celebrating one half of that picture and dismissing the other as a word his opponents made up.

Georgia gubernatorial candidate Rick Jackson, who spoke alongside Trump, offered his own reframing of the affordability debate: "The other side talks about affordability as if that's a solution. Affordability is not a political problem. Affordability is a business problem"—a remarkable statement for many reasons, not least for undercutting Trump's tendency to blame high prices on Joe Biden.

Trump's comments about the price of oil told a similar story.

When Trump turned to energy prices, he framed recent spikes as temporary friction on the way to a much larger drop. "Oil will be coming—it's going to be tumbling down," he told the crowd, describing a collapsed deal with Iran three weeks earlier: "They thought we had a deal. I said, 'I don't think we have a deal with these guys. They break every deal they make.'"

The next day, oil surged on renewed fighting in the Middle East. Brent crude rose more than 6% to over $100 per barrel in early Thursday trading, its highest level since the end of May, while U.S. crude jumped for a second straight day to almost $92—its highest since early June.

On Wednesday night, Trump had insisted prices would eventually fall—"maybe lower than when we even started"—but asked supporters for patience: "Just give me a little time."

The escalation also collided with Trump's framing of the conflict as a mere "skirmish" that Iran wants to end. Houthi attacks on Saudi Arabian oil tankers in the Red Sea forced at least five vessels to reroute around the Bab el-Mandeb Strait to avoid the danger zone. "They're getting hit so hard, and they want to make a deal," Trump said. "But I say they're not ready to make a deal, because every time they make a deal, they want to change it."

Buried in the speech was a moment of concession Trump rarely offers on cost-of-living messaging. "I just want you to remember that if you're paying a little bit more, a lot of it's come down now. We got it coming down very rapidly, actually," he said. Trump seemed to be talking about the slowing rate of inflation, which is coming down, while voters nationwide are angry that prices actually haven't gone into reverse after years of what they see as unfair profit-taking. Since Trump told voters that he'd "won" on affordability in February—during another rally in Georgia, in fact—his polling numbers have served as a strong counterargument. The latest polling available shows a more than 30-point plunge in voter opinion on his handling of the economy since his inauguration, with the net rating for his handling of inflation and prices at -43, the lowest for this term.

Trump held up falling egg prices as proof that his approach works, recalling that Easter egg hunt organizers once suggested using "plastic eggs" because real ones were too expensive. He credited his agriculture secretary with getting egg prices "lower than … when we started"—a claim that lines up with independent data showing egg prices down nearly 28% year over year, though economists attribute the drop primarily to flock recovery after avian flu outbreaks rather than administration policy.

Several weeks earlier, the Justice Department and 17 states reached settlement agreements with three major egg producers this week over allegations that they had illegally colluded for years to raise prices. Cal-Maine—the only public company among the settling parties—reported a profit of $1.22 billion for the 2025 fiscal year; it agreed to pay $1.5 million and donate 30 million eggs, without admitting wrongdoing.

Oil is a different story. Unlike eggs, crude prices are hostage to an active shooting conflict Trump has repeatedly declared nearly over, only to see it reignite. He told the Marietta crowd that Venezuela was now helping fill the gap—claiming the U.S. and Venezuela together control "about 62% of the world's oil market" and don't "need the Hormuz Strait"—but added: "We do it because we have to do it, because we cannot let Iran have a nuclear weapon." Another source disagreed with this statement: President Trump himself, who in January estimated the U.S. and Venezuela have 55% of the world's oil market. In fact, BP and Energy Information Administration (EIA) data indicate both of those are massive overstatements, and the true percentage is roughly 24%.

In other remarks in Marietta, Trump seemed to celebrate business as something that isn't problematic at all, noting that the stock market has hit 73 all-time highs since the election and that the typical American's 401(k) is up by $34,000, "setting records day after day." What he left unmentioned is a divergence Wall Street's own research desks have been flagging for months. J.P. Morgan Private Bank found that stock valuations are trading near record highs while consumer confidence sits close to record lows, attributing the gap to what strategist Jason Seydl called "the inescapable word—affordability." The University of Michigan's Consumer Sentiment Index should be running near 93 based on stock performance, unemployment, and inflation trends alone; instead it's roughly 40 points lower, closer to historic lows.

The mismatch traces to who actually owns the rally. Much of the 2026 market surge has been concentrated in a handful of AI-driven megacaps, whose earnings gains rarely translate into higher household spending power. Meanwhile, median-income families now need roughly $50,000 

For this story, Fortune journalists used generative AI as a research tool. An editor verified the accuracy of the information before publishing.

This story was originally featured on Fortune.com

title: Ultrahuman’s New App Fixes Most of What Frustrated Me About the Old One
description: The "Emerald Update" makes it easier to see what I'm supposed to do with my data.
fullText:
When I first reviewed an Ultrahuman ring (the Ring Air, last year), I wrote that my biggest complaint was that I found the app overbearing. To give an example of but one of the things that annoyed me about it, it scored me on a metric called “brain waste clearance,” as if that is something it could measure or that I could affect. But a new version of the app—Ultrahuman is calling it the Emerald Update—changes everything.

I’ve been using a beta version of this new app as I test the forthcoming Ring Pro, and so far, I'm impressed: It's now easier to navigate, and presents the data from my ring in a way that’s actually useful and easy to read.

The first time I open the new Ultrahuman app each day, I see a screen that gives a small amount of information—just the important stuff—about how I slept and how my day is shaping up. For example, today it says my sleep is improving, my resting heart rate is normal, and my temperature is “borderline” (a little higher than usual). It tells me I only got six hours of sleep. That’s pretty much everything I want to know when I check the app early in the day.

On the home screen (what I see after I dismiss that initial greeting), the Ultrahuman app now distills its firehose of data into a few “next best actions.” The top one for me right now is to get up and move, since I’ve been pretty sedentary today. If I tap to see more, the next recommendation is to set an alarm to start my bedtime routine, since my bedtime has been inconsistent. These recommendations are actually pretty useful!

The old Ultrahuman app had tabs at the bottom of the screen, but most of them were useless unless you had purchased specific Ultrahuman products (blood testing, glucose monitors, or a home air quality monitor). Now, there are five tabs: the ring tab, with all my main data screens; “longevity,” which gives me an “ultra age” showing how fast I am supposedly aging according to my habits; Jade, which I’ll say more about in a minute; PowerPlugs, where you can choose add-ons that give you extra features (some free, some subscription-based); and “zones,” a social feature (a zone is apparently something like a group or club).

I find the longevity tab useful for longer-term metrics. I don’t put much stock in its exact longevity number (what does it mean to be two years older than I am?), but this tab also shows my estimated VO2max, along with a note explaining a recent algorithm update. As I scroll down, I can see some recommendations for “slow[ing] my aging,” which amount to advice for long-term health—mine is that I need to tackle my sleep debt (I also have low “blood flow smoothness,” apparently). The app’s mystifying ways haven’t disappeared entirely; they’re just a little further from the surface.

“Jade” houses an AI chatbot that I honestly try to interact with as little as possible. The card telling me about my blood flow smoothness suggested that I ask Jade what this means and how to improve it. I tapped the button to do so, and Jade told me it doesn’t know what blood flow smoothness is. This part seems to need some more work.

In the old version of the app, I was constantly being told when and when not to consume caffeine. This is still a big part of the app, though you can disable it by uninstalling the free caffeine PowerPlug that is on by default. Now, however, the app shows me my “active windows” at any given time. Right now, it's the afternoon, and the app tells me it’s fine to have caffeine, that sunlight and exercise won’t affect my sleep timing, and that it’s OK to look at screens. Later this evening, the caffeine window will close, and later, my screen time window will close.

The app doesn’t do a great job of explaining these windows, however. What exactly will happen when “phase delay window” starts? But I do like that there’s now a view that shows all three timelines in parallel, labeled with different times. That’s not to say I trust the underlying decisions any more—I’m still definitely going to stop having caffeine well before 6 p.m., but I also know the evidence doesn’t actually support skipping my morning coffee.

Overall, I’ve found the experience of using the ring a lot more pleasant alongside the new app redesign. Ultrahuman says that data processing now happens on-device, so you don’t need an internet connection to get your metrics and recommendations. I’m still working my way through the app’s features as I test the Ring Pro, but I’m looking forward to the improved heart rate accuracy and the ability to use data from Garmin and other devices for workouts. There’s even a feature to “train your ring” by using it alongside another device. Sometimes, change is good.

title: This App Can Help Detect When Someone Near You Is Wearing Meta Glasses
description: But it only detects them when they are charging, powering on, or while they're actively pairing.
fullText:
An app has launched that detects whether the people around you are using Meta and other kinds of smart glasses. Antizuck Smart Glasses Scanner, available for iPhone and designed by Albert Pariente Cohen, detects nearby Bluetooth signals that match Meta glasses, Snap Spectacles, Meta Quest headsets, RayNeo smart glasses, and other devices; then, it lets you know what gadget is nearby and whether it's been detected previously. In keeping with the "privacy" focus, Antizuck doesn't require an account or email address, nor does it send any data outside of your phone. It retails for $2.99 with no subscription.

In my testing, Antizuck successfully identified both my Meta glasses and my Quest 3 headset. It actually called out my Quest 3 (name and distance) while the headset was in sleep mode. The Meta glasses, though, weren't as easily detected. While it did find my glasses, it only "saw" them when I turned them off and on: Antizuck only detects Meta glasses that are charging, powering on, or while they're actively pairing.

The app can also tell you all the other Bluetooth devices advertising around you, and it identifies what they are, if possible. Interestingly, it identified my charging Even Realities G2 smart glasses, but didn't flag them, I assume because they don't have a camera. Still, just seeing how many devices are broadcasting around you is worth the $3 price of admission.

It's clear from the app's "glasses spotted" warning of "Someone may be recording near you" that the point is to let you know if a creep is creeping nearby, but they're only marginally useful for that purpose. They do not let you know which passersby are wearing Meta glasses. They don't show you an alert when a nearby pair of glasses starts recording or takes a picture, nor do they alert you when a nearby pair of Meta glasses is transferring media from the glasses to a phone. If someone turns on their glasses near you or charges them for three minutes or so, you'll get a ping and a record of whether that same pair of glasses has been detected by you in the past. But most people aren't charging their glasses on a street corner or in a bar.

Utility aside, the app is clearly part of the growing backlash against people using camera-forward smart glasses, whether it's New York banning them in court, or singer Lorde calling them "not sexy." I completely understand the concern people, particularly women, have about being recorded by hidden spy glasses, but it's not as one-sided an argument as it may appear at first. People with vision problems use Meta glasses to get verbal help from the AI. Birdwatchers use them to identify birds on the fly. I use my Meta glasses to categorize receipts for tax purposes, listen to Hawkwind, and occasionally take videos on bike rides. I'd rather not have strangers assume I'm a creep, or know that I'm wearing a specific brand of eyeglasses at all. Just as it's legal to take photos in public places, it's legal to scan your surroundings for Bluetooth signatures, but isn't each an invasion of privacy? Like anything, the only thing evil about a tool is how you use it.

title: Sex work or starve: Aid workers in Nepal left jobless by USAID cuts turn to sex work to survive
description: She paces the side of the darkened highway in central Nepal, just enough light from the trucks roaring past to illuminate her painted face to the men rolling in on rickshaws and motorbikes.  Rubi Lama...
fullText:
HETAUDA, Nepal (AP) — She paces the side of the darkened highway in central Nepal, just enough light from the trucks roaring past to illuminate her painted face to the men rolling in on rickshaws and motorbikes. She knows that every stranger who pays for her body could end her life. But she also knows their money is the only thing preventing her death.

This spot, littered in condom wrappers and fringed by jungle, is familiar to her. Rubi Lama once came here not as a sex worker, but as an aid worker who helped sex workers.

For nine years, Lama — who is transgender — had devoted her life to conducting HIV outreach in Nepal's marginalized communities, including the LGBTQ+ sex workers who wait by this highway each night. But after the United States slashed its foreign aid funding last year, Lama and more than 280,000 aid workers worldwide found themselves out of a job.

Much has been written about the devastating impacts the U.S. foreign aid cuts have had on the millions of people the funding once helped. Far less is known about the impacts on the helpers themselves.

In impoverished Nepal, where a conservative culture leaves openly transgender people with few legal job options, the impact on aid workers' lives was particularly crushing. Starving and scared, Lama and around 100 other LGBTQ+ aid workers left jobless by the funding cuts have turned to sex work to survive, walking the streets alongside the very people they once fought so hard to help.

The public health implications are vast. With the U.S. funding cuts also eliminating access to HIV prevention medication in Nepal, health experts fear a spike in HIV prevalence among the surging ranks of sex workers, as well as members of the LGBTQ+ community.

On the side of the highway, the night is a cacophony of blaring horns and the shouts of drunken men. Lama, 28 years old, softspoken and shy, walks quietly among them, equal parts desperation and dread. Beyond her fear of HIV is her fear of violence, which she and nearly all her former colleagues who have taken to the streets have endured.

She'd give anything to go back to the nonprofit where she once cared for society's most vulnerable. Now she is the one in need of care, but there is no one left to provide it.

It is 11 p.m. and she's had two customers so far, who each paid her $3. Rent and food this month will cost her $80.

And so, her slight frame nearly swallowed by the shadows, she waits.

Just a few minutes down the road, in Lama's old office, stickers bearing the logo of the U.S. Agency for International Development still cling to laptops that lie unused on barren desks, and to the empty condom distribution containers collecting dust.

This is the headquarters for the formerly USAID-funded non-governmental organization called Friends Hetauda, which offered HIV testing, counseling and prevention services to central Nepal's LGBTQ+ community. All of that ended in January 2025, when U.S. President Donald Trump froze funding for USAID, once the world's leading donor of humanitarian assistance. Trump later dissolved the agency after calling it a waste of money, even though the U.S. spent just 1% of its budget on foreign aid.

In a statement to The Associated Press, the U.S. State Department said the Trump administration had reinvigorated its assistance programs to focus on efficiency, effectiveness and partnership. The department also said the U.S. spends more on health and humanitarian assistance than any other country.

"The rest of the world needs to contribute more and share the burden," the department said.

Of the more than 281,000 jobs lost due to Trump's dismantling of USAID, just 23,000 were American, says Sadie Healy, who runs a global health consulting firm and has been tracking the job losses worldwide. The rest were overseas — many of them belonging to impoverished locals living in conflict zones or economically depressed regions where other employment options are scarce.

In Nepal alone, an estimated 35,000 aid workers lost their jobs amid the USAID cuts, says NGO Federation of Nepal President Arjun Bhattarai. With the country already facing high unemployment, the fallout was devastating.

The Blue Diamond Society, an umbrella organization for NGOs like Lama's former employer, received 85% of its funding from USAID and was staffed largely by people who were themselves gender and sexual minorities. They served as counselors, health assistants, data collectors and outreach workers. They ran 20 clinics across Nepal, dispensing antiretroviral medication to people with HIV along with PrEP, a medication taken by HIV-negative people to prevent infection. They offered free HIV tests along with condoms and lubricant, which reduce the risk of HIV transmission.

The funding cuts shut down the society's services, leaving more than 1,200 clients without access to PrEP medication and 262 of the group's 350 staffers without work.

Outside of NGOs, legal work for openly transgender people in Nepal is virtually nonexistent, executive director Manisha Dhakal says. Which is why between 35% and 45% of the society's former staffers turned to sex work, she says.

The risks are immense, she says, for both her former employees and the public. Too fearful of discrimination to go to hospitals for antiretroviral drugs, many transgender people have stopped taking them — significantly increasing their risk of transmission. Lubricant and condoms are expensive, so some are having unprotected sex. And PrEP distribution has been halted to everyone but pregnant and lactating women.

"One decision from one powerful country — all these services are cut," Dhakal says.

She sits at the society's now-hushed headquarters, the bookshelf behind her lined with framed certificates from USAID, commending her organization for saving lives. Now, she says, those lives are on the line.

"The Trump administration's executive order will cause people to die," she says.

Her fears are echoed by Dr. Sarbesh Sharma, director of Nepal's National Centre for AIDS and STD Control. Viruses like HIV, he warns, do not respect borders.

"Borders are for humans," he says. "We have a lot of migrant workers, we have a lot of overseas students, we have an open border with India. So those make it not only a Nepal concern, it can become a global concern."

As a result of the drop in global aid, HIV testing fell by 22% in countries with high infection rates, while funding for condoms was cut by more than 90% in some places, according to data released in June by the United Nations agency that fights HIV.

All 15 aid workers-turned-sex workers interviewed by the AP recognize the risks. One, who is HIV positive, says she lives in fear of transmitting the disease, yet still hits the streets nearly every night to stave off starvation.

Most of the workers spent months trying to find other jobs, frequently being rejected the moment the employers realized they were transgender. Many have been forced to have unprotected sex with clients. Nearly all have faced violence, including gang rape.

Aashika Tamang, who spent 16 years working in HIV prevention, longs for the days where she liaised with volunteers and social workers. Now, she says, she is abused at least once a week and largely liaises with addicts.

There's a surreal whiplash when she bumps into fellow sex workers who once came to her for help.

"I used to meet them during the day," she says. "Now I meet them during the night."

This story was funded by a grant from the Pulitzer Center. The Associated Press is responsible for all content.

On her first night as a sex worker, Lama sat in her apartment, frozen by indecision and fear. Her makeup was on and her wig was in place, but her mind was in meltdown.

Brainy and warm, with a gentle smile and soft brown eyes rimmed by glasses, she — like most of her former colleagues — had battled so many barriers on her path to aid work. Raised by a conservative family amid crippling poverty, she spent her childhood selling eggs in the local market to pay for her first years of school, where a teacher sexually assaulted her.

Lama finished high school thanks to a government scholarship for impoverished students. She then studied education at a university, but was unable to finish her degree amid harassment from teachers and classmates.

Her salvation came in 2016, when she was recruited by Friends Hetauda, named for the industrial transit city where it is based. Lama searched for, counseled and tested those at high risk of HIV, including the sex workers who service a stream of travelers.

Her father, furious over her job and gender identity, kicked her out. But Lama had found her calling; she knew she was saving lives. A series of promotions eventually netted her around 30,000 Nepalese rupees ($200) per month — enough to rent a one-room apartment.

And then one day in February 2025, she walked into her office and ran into Friends Hetauda's coordinator, Kiran Thapa. The group's funding, Thapa told her, was gone. Of its 17 staffers, the U.S. aid cuts had left Lama and 13 others out of a job.

Lama was terrified. She couldn't go home; her father had made that clear. How would she survive?

In the months that followed, she watched with worry as her peers' mental health worsened and as more began testing positive for HIV.

She applied for local government jobs she was initially told she had, only to be rejected once she showed up in person. A hotel manager offered her a position as a cleaner, then grabbed her breasts during a training session. The job was hers, he told her — but only if she had sex with him and his friends. Lama refused and was dismissed.

She started skipping meals and could no longer afford medicine or rent. Her landlord threatened to throw her out.

And so she began talking to her former colleagues about their new roles in the sex industry. Of the 14 Friends Hetauda staffers who lost their jobs, between 8 and 10 moved to sex work, says Thapa.

Lama realized it was sex work or starve. She knew where to go: the dark spot by the highway where she'd once tested sex workers and their clients for HIV.

For two nights, she hung back and observed. On her third night, she committed herself to landing a client. She began getting ready around 8 p.m., putting on her makeup and wig, along with a traditional white kurta and red leggings. By 9 p.m., she looked the part, but didn't feel it.

And so she sat, paralyzed, in her apartment.

At 11:30, she forced herself down to the highway. After two hours, the first man approached. "You wanna go?" he asked. She didn't, but climbed onto his motorbike anyway, after he promised to pay her 1,000 rupees ($6.50).

At his home, she was sick with fear. "Why do I have to do this?" she thought. "Is this really my path?"

When it was over, he shortchanged her, paying her just 700 rupees ($4.50), and dropped her along the highway.

Back at home, she washed off her makeup and crawled into bed. But sleep wouldn't come.

Gripped by anxiety, her body grew hot and her thoughts grew dark. She thought about death, and whether it was the kinder alternative.

She thought, too, about her old job. That work had been her life's purpose.

In the unlit alleys, dense jungles and private homes where the workers service their clients, safety does not exist.

"All of my sex worker friends, including myself, who are involved in this profession are victims of violence," Lama says.

Ironically, the workers say, it is their powers of persuasion and knowledge of the risks — skills honed through their counseling and outreach roles — that sometimes save them.

Lama's training helped her remain outwardly calm the night a man pulled a knife on her, held it to her throat and threatened to cut her unless she had sex with him. She bought time, telling him he'd be at risk of HIV if he raped her, given how many other customers she'd been with. Then she shoved him and ran to a bathroom near a gas station, hiding there until friends rescued her.

A few hours' drive to the north, in the capital, Kathmandu, Sujata Chaudhary frequently draws on her 11 years of work in HIV prevention to explain the dangers of unprotected sex to clients who try to force it upon her. Sometimes, she says, the men back down. Sometimes they don't. She contracted syphilis as a result.

One night, she managed to talk her way out of a house where two men were threatening to release their large dogs on her. Another night, she confronted several police officers who had beaten her with their batons as she stood near a tea vendor, calling on her knowledge of the law to challenge them.

But often the abuse is impossible to stop. Two men once picked up Chaudhary and a friend and drove them against their will to a hostel an hour away, where more than a dozen other men were waiting. Chaudhary, slender and sensitive, with a tattoo on her arm that says "Aama," or mother, in honor of the woman she likens to God, was no match for them. She begged them to stop, but was raped by at least eight men. She and her friend were eventually released, and forced to walk hours back to town.

She refuses to go to customers' homes or hotel rooms now. But she has to keep working. Her father died years ago, leaving her family dependent on her income. She cannot bring herself to tell them, including her Aama, how she earns it.

Barsha Magar, who served at Friends Hetauda for 16 years as a peer educator, outreach worker and data collector, feared she would be killed when she transitioned to sex work. Two months into the job, she nearly was. Two soldiers who initially seemed kind took her into the jungle, held her at gunpoint and raped her while holding her mouth shut to stifle her screams.

She wipes away tears as she recalls the terror of that night. For two weeks, she was too traumatized to return to the streets.

For Bharati Thapa, a former Friends Hetauda field supervisor and peer educator, the violence started the very first night she stood along the highway. Two Indian tourists picked her up and took her to a guest house, where another eight men were waiting, all of them drunk. She was physically assaulted and raped before fleeing the house, clothing torn, body covered in scratches, and screaming for a guard to let her out through the locked gates.

She sits one recent evening in the darkened office of Friends Hetauda.

"My life is at risk now," she says softly. "I fear that I may contract HIV and sexually transmitted infections. I fear being beaten up and harassed by customers. I fear the police taking me away at night. How can I not be afraid? There is risk from all sides."

Back on the side of the highway, Lama has landed another customer and disappeared into the dark.

It is Friday, the busiest night of the week. Around 1 a.m., the police arrive, wielding long batons. The sex workers scatter, including Lama. It is time to go home, she says. She has made a total of 1,600 rupees ($10.50) from five clients. It's a relatively good haul; sometimes she goes home with nothing.

"I wish it was fair," she says. "I wish I could live with my identity. I wish I could walk in the way that makes me happy. I know what is right and wrong. I am aware of the road I am taking."

But without a new funding source, she says, there is no way off this road. And so this is where she will return, night after night, to stand alongside the women she once tried to save, in a bid to save herself.

Journalist Tulsi Rauniyar contributed to this report.

title: Navy revises what information about sailors can be released, citing internal misinterpretation
description: Citing an internal misinterpretation of a change in policy, the U.S. Navy is backing away from statements that it would begin to restrict press and public access to sailors' service histories — inform...
fullText:
WASHINGTON (AP) — Citing an internal misinterpretation of a change in policy, the U.S. Navy is backing away from statements that it would begin to restrict press and public access to sailors' service histories — information that has been freely offered for decades.

"The Navy continues to fulfill service record requests under our longstanding, standard disclosure procedures," the Navy said in a statement hours after The Associated Press reported a policy change that meant sailor biographies would be off limits to the press.

On Monday, Capt. Candice Tresch, a spokeswoman for the chief of Naval personnel, told the AP that the service would "no longer provide public, on-demand access to Sailor biographies, unless the subject of the request is a public figure or extenuating circumstances exist." She explained the policy in the wake of the Navy's denial of a request by The Associated Press for a specific sailor's biography.

A Navy official, who spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss a sensitive situation, said Thursday afternoon that Tresch's statement was the result of a misinterpretation of a recently released Navy policy that has now been resolved.

Last week, the Navy's top spokesman, Rear Admiral John Robinson, released a policy message that mandated the sea service remove all public-facing information about its commanding officers. It was this policy that the Navy official now says was misinterpreted — and that the actual policy of the Navy on disclosing sailor biographies never actually changed.

Tresch said the Navy's personnel office decided to expand the policy to deny access to any currently serving sailor's service information, in the interest of equality. She said that while they would still confirm whether someone is or previously was a sailor, no other details would be offered until the undefined threat had passed.

Robinson's office did not respond to emails on both Tuesday and Wednesday with questions about how the Chief of Naval Personnel's office was interpreting Robinson's policy. But in a statement posted Thursday on X, Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell said there had been no change in the Navy's policy on releasing service records for sailors. He said it merely concerned leadership biographies.

"There has been NO change to the Navy's policies governing the release of service records," Parnell wrote. "To enhance the security of our Sailors and their families, Naval Administrative Message (NAVADMIN) 170/26 updated the policy for leadership biographies on public-facing websites — not service records."

He added: "The Navy continues to review requests for service records on a case-by-case basis. Every single media request has been fulfilled since the NAVADMIN was issued."

Tresch described the new policy, however, in the wake of the Navy denying a request from The Associated Press for a specific sailor biography. The Navy's latest statement was provided to the AP several hours after Parnell's post.

Information about sailors' service history is critical in providing information and context when service members are involved in major news events. It is also information that a constellation of federal laws and Pentagon policies say should be made available on request.

The Pentagon's governing document on its privacy program notes that information like a service member's rank, duty assignments, awards, and service status is categorized as information "that is normally releasable" and "may be disclosed without a clearly unwarranted invasion of their personal privacy." However, according to Tresch, the Navy will now only release these details if the sailor is "a public figure or extenuating circumstances exist."

The Navy cited the new policy last week in denying the AP's request for the biography of a pilot with the Blue Angels, the Navy's demonstration squadron, following a controversial maneuver over a crowded beach. Robinson's office wouldn't provide the pilot's biography despite the fact that his name, photo, and other service details were posted on the official Blue Angels website. Tresch did provide a copy of the biography personally after the AP began inquiring about the policy.

Last month, the military asked Congress for the ability to withhold certain types of unclassified records. The legislative proposal would create a new section of federal law that would allow the defense secretary to exempt certain "controlled unclassified information," or CUI, from disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act.

Transparency advocates, and even the Pentagon's own internal watchdog, have described the term CUI as inconsistently applied and overused. Critics have argued that it is arbitrarily used to keep embarrassing information from being made public.

Last year, the Pentagon attempted to impose restrictions on journalists working inside the Pentagon, which, in turn, led most news outlets to turn in their access badges and walk out. The policy is now being litigated in federal court. This summer, the Pentagon went a step further and declared that its press office was now a classified space.

This story has been updated to correct the name of the Chief of Naval Personnel spokeswoman, Capt. Candice Tresch, not Thresh.

title: Turns out Dead Internet Theory was right: AI agents are eating the Web, growing by nearly 8,000% and rewiring the Internet’s business model
description: Multiple cyber firms agree: bots outnumber humans online, challenging advertising, analytics and security systems designed around human visitors.
fullText:
The dead internet theory has floated around internet forums since the 2010s, and the idea that the internet is dominated by non-human activity was often dismissed as a fringe conspiracy. Now it has become a measurable fact. Not only do multiple cybersecurity firms agree that bots outnumber humans online, they likewise are struggling to answer when the flip happened or what metrics to use to measure it.

How have bots overtaken humans in web traffic?

What challenges remain for the machine economy?

What are AI agents and agentic browsers?

How are companies adapting to agent-driven traffic?

CloudFlare, the Internet security and performance giant used by millions of websites worldwide, says the crossover occurred in June, with bots generating 57.5% of webpage requests. Thales, a French tech group that protects data security for organizations and governments, dates the crossover back to 2023, issuing a "Bad Bot Report" that put bot traffic at 53% in 2026.

The discrepancies reflect that there's no one standard way to measure bot traffic, as no single provider has access to activity across the entire web, according to Rudy Yang, Pitchbook's enterprise and retail fintech analyst who wrote the firm's July report about agentic AI traffic.

"There's a lot of missing pieces of information, but a lot of the observed data suggests the same thing, which is like there is more bot activity," Yang told Fortune. "Agentic AI activity is driving a lot of the browser activity you're seeing."

The surge of agents is massive everywhere you look. Traffic generated by agents that actually take action on the web, like clicking links and filling out forms, grew 7,851% year over year, according to cybersecurity firm HUMAN Security's 2026 State of AI Traffic & Cyberthreat Benchmark Report. Scraper traffic, by comparison, grew 597% over the same stretch, and AI training crawlers, while still 67.5% of AI-driven traffic, are a shrinking share of the total.

The timing caught even bot-watchers off guard. CloudFlare CEO Matthew Prince had predicted in March that bots wouldn't cross the halfway mark until the end of 2027. Instead, the crossover arrived more than a year early.

"For companies and developers, this means that building for agent traffic will become nonnegotiable," Yang wrote in the report.

The Internet's business model—ad impressions, conversion funnels, pageview-based analytics— was built on the assumption that the visitor is a human being. If most of them are now agents, that assumption is upended and reshapes how companies monetize web traffic The same autonomy driving that agentic AI traffic is what let an OpenAI model slip its cage this week—meaning the volume surge is just the visible symptom of a shift businesses can neither fully measure nor, yet, fully control.

title: Hull KR inflict more derby misery on Hull FC
description: Out-of-form Super League champions Hull KR put a stop to their losing run as they battle back to beat city rivals Hull FC in a feisty derby.
fullText:
Tries: Batchelor, D Litten, Arthur Goals: Hardaker 4

Tries: Amone, Mourgue, Broadbent, Burgess, Dezaria Goals: Mourgue 7

Out-of-form Super League champions Hull KR put a stop to their losing run as they battled back to beat city rivals Hull FC in a feisty derby.

The struggling home side, who have now won just one of their past 11 games, raced into a 12-0 lead in the opening 10 minutes thanks to tries from Joe Batchelor and Davy Litten, but scores from Tom Amone and Arthur Mourgue levelled the game at the break.

Jack Broadbent's try put the Robins in front for the first time on the night and Joe Burgess raced in for a 95-metre intercept try to give the away side breathing room.

Jake Arthur gave the hosts a glimmer of hope from Zak Hardaker's wonderful grubber kick, but Jordan Dezaria powered over to cap the win as the Robins moved back into the play-off positions in fifth.

In what was Willie Peters' final derby game as KR coach, his side extended their winning run to nine in a row against their rivals.

Despite the bright start, Thursday's result was a sixth straight loss for second-bottom Hull FC, whose miserable season continues.

This was the second derby between the sides this month after they met at Everton's Hill Dickinson Stadium three weeks ago in a Magic Weekend game in which the Robins had to dig deep to win 26-12.

Off the back of defeats by Wakefield Trinity and Warrington Wolves since that success, the Robins got off to the worst possible start when they lost their captain's challenge inside 90 seconds and then fell two scores behind after Batchelor went in from Aidan Sezer's line-breaking run and Davy Litten did well to ground the ball under pressure.

The visitors, who claimed the World Club Challenge against Brisbane Broncos in their last game at the MKM Stadium in February, could have gone under.

However, they regained their composure and Amone barrelled over after good work from Jez Litten before Mourgue, in for the suspended Mikey Lewis, scored as the teams went in 12-12 at the break.

After a Hardaker penalty put the home side back in the lead after a high tackle on Lennon Clark, Broadbent climbed highest to grab a Jez Litten kick and battled over to put Peters' men on top for the first time.

Burgess, back in the 13 after being left out of the squad entirely last week, intercepted a Jake Arthur pass five metres from his own line and held off a tackle from Logan Moy to run in and put KR in charge.

A helter-skelter passage of play ended with Hardaker showing brilliant football skills to tee up Arthur to reduce the arrears after a superb offload by Harvey Barron, but a comeback was not on the cards and Frenchman Dezaria powered over from close range for his first try for the Robins.

Hull FC, whose owners put out a statement earlier in the week accepting responsibility for their wretched campaign, head to bottom club Huddersfield Giants next Thursday, while KR welcome Bradford Bulls next Friday.

Hull FC interim coach Andy Last told BBC Radio Humberside:

"Really disappointed, it would have been great to get two points. Unbelievable crowd came in on the back of a season where we have really struggled. The loyalty of the supporters is second to none.

"We got off to a great start. We felt the start was important for us to get that engagement with the supporters and we thought Rovers were a bit fragile after their last two performances, so it couldn't have gone any better.

"The tries we conceded were real soft ones and that was down to fatigue.

"Second half, Rovers just did what they do which is suffocate you of field position. I ended up with a bit of a neck ache because I was looking to my left all the time. That pressure they put you under forced us into some uncharacteristic errors."

Hull KR head coach Willie Peters told BBC Humberside:

"I'm really proud. The first 10 minutes was a nervous start, 12 [points] down, it could have got ugly but we fought.

"I thought Tom Amone and Kyle Lawton were outstanding. They made a big difference when they came on. I say it every week but what Dean Hadley brings to our team is incredible."

[On Elliot Minchella and Jez Litten] "When times get a little bit tough you want your leaders to stand up and that is what I thought both of those guys did tonight and put in great performances.

"I said before the game that I thought this was the perfect game for us to show we've still got that fight. Being 12-0 down at your rivals to be able to kick on at the back end was what we needed."

Hull FC: Moy; Barron, Hardaker, Litten, Briscoe; Arthur, Sezer; Salabio, Cust, Hill, Batchelor, O'Neill, Bell.

Interchanges: Bourouh, Clark, Ese'ese, Wood.

Hull KR: Broadbent; Davies, Hiku, Gildart, Burgess; Mourgue, May; Sue, Litten, Hadley, Whitbread, Batchelor, Minchella.

Interchanges: Amone, Luckley, Lawton, Dezaria.

title: 'We contributed to our own downfall' - Higgins
description: Coleraine manager Ruaidhrí Higgins says his side "contributed" to their own downfall in Thursday's 5-0 away defeat by HJK in the first leg of their Uefa Conference League second qualifying round tie.
fullText:
Coleraine manager Ruaidhrí Higgins says his side "contributed" to their own downfall in Thursday's 5-0 away defeat by HJK in the first leg of their Uefa Conference League second qualifying round tie.

The Bannsiders were two down at the break through goals from Liam Moller and Pyry Mentu, while Toivo Mero added a third early in the second have with a quickfire brace by Teemu Pukki completing the rout.

Higgins expressed his "disappointment" at the manner of the defeat but had no complaints as they now have a mountain to climb in next week's second leg at home.

"The manner of the goals we gave away and the timing were extremely disappointing," Higgins told Coleraine TV.

"We contributed to a lot of their goals and that's on us, so we have to accept it."

A bad night for Coleraine was made worse as Aidan Wilson and Mark Coyle were forced off with what Higgins described as "serious injuries".

However, Higgins was not using injuries or his team's fitness levels being below HJK, who are in the middle of their domestic campaign, as an excuse.

"They are in their prime of fitness and we're just searching for fitness," he offered.

"Lads like [Joel] Cooper and [Will] Patching have had very little football in pre-season. Cameron Stewart picked up an injury last week, then two defensive-minded players like Aidan Wilson and Mark Coyle pick up what looks like serious injuries.

"We were a bit makeshift, a bit disjointed, but we still have to do better as they didn't have to do much to score their goals.

"We have to learn from that and learn fast, take it on the chin and try to move on as fast as we can."

title: Bodycam shows Connecticut officer repeatedly ordering bat-wielding suspect to stop before fatal shooting
description: Body camera footage shows a Meriden police officer ordering Robert Lee Jenkins III to drop a baseball bat before firing as the suspect bludgeoned a victim.
fullText:
A Connecticut police officer repeatedly ordered a man to drop a baseball bat before opening fire as the suspect continued bludgeoning a motionless man in the head, newly released body camera footage shows.

The footage, released this week by the Connecticut Office of Inspector General, captures the July 19 encounter in Meriden that left two men dead, including 38-year-old Robert Lee Jenkins III, who investigators said was shot by a responding officer while carrying out the attack.

According to the inspector general's preliminary report, Meriden Officer Anthony Offiaeli arrived at a home on Sagamore Road shortly after 10 a.m. and found Jenkins "bludgeoning" a man lying motionless on the front lawn with a baseball bat.

Video Shows Undercover Cops Shooting Man Threatening Shoppers With Fake Gun In 'Justified' Defense: Officials

"Put the f---ing bat down!" the officer shouted repeatedly while pointing his firearm at Jenkins, body camera footage shows.

He then radioed for backup, urging responding officers to "step it up."

Follow The Fox True Crime Team On X

As Offiaeli advanced, Jenkins swung the baseball bat downward four times, appearing to strike the victim on the ground with each blow.

The officer fired multiple shots, but Jenkins continued striking the victim with the bat. The officer fired additional rounds before Jenkins began to slow down, though he continued swinging the bat until he eventually collapsed.

Like What You're Reading? Click Here For More

The inspector general's preliminary report states Offiaeli ordered Jenkins to drop the baseball bat four times before opening fire after Jenkins continued striking the victim.

Jenkins received medical treatment at the scene before being taken to Hartford Hospital, where he died at 11:30 p.m., investigators said.

The victim who was attacked with the baseball bat was pronounced dead at the scene. His identity has not been released because Connecticut law prohibits disclosing the identity of victims in family violence cases.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

An autopsy determined Jenkins died from multiple gunshot wounds to the torso, and the manner of death was ruled a homicide, according to the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner.

The victim's cause of death was determined to be blunt force head injuries, and the manner of death was also ruled a homicide. Toxicology results and Jenkins' death certificate were still pending as of Tuesday.

In an initial statement following the shooting, Meriden Police Chief Roberto Rosado said the incident "reminds us that the dangers of Police work is real" and that there was no ongoing threat to the public.

The Connecticut Office of Inspector General, Connecticut State Police Central District Major Crime Squad, the Meriden Police Department and the New Haven Judicial District State's Attorney's Office are continuing to investigate the incident.

title: Thune responds to Leavitt quip that Trump’s patience with him ‘running out’ over SAVE America Act
description: Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) bristled when asked Thursday about White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt’s statement that President Trump’s “patience is running out” with the Senate ...
fullText:
Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) bristled when asked Thursday about White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt's statement that President Trump's "patience is running out" with the Senate GOP leader because of the stalled SAVE America Act.

"Maybe she or somebody else ought to get on the phone and get the votes. Right? It's 50 around here," Thune shot back when asked about Leavitt's statement to reporters at a press briefing earlier Thursday.

"Instead of pointing the finger at Republicans, they might think about going after the people who are stopping it on the floor, which is the Democrats," the senator added, appearing somewhat irritated by the critical comment. "And if there are Republicans they think are gettable, get on the phone."

Leavitt said Trump's patience with Thune's handling of the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility (SAVE America) Act, which the Senate has voted on — in whole or in part — at least five times but has failed to move forward, is wearing thin.

The remark comes as the president has ramped up his push for Republicans to support the voter ID bill, legislation that would require proof of citizenship for voter registration and photo ID when casting ballots.

"The president spoke about this directly yesterday," she told reporters. "His patience is running out.

"He wants to see as much of the SAVE America passed as possible by the August recess. He knows that's what the American people want to see passed. He was just traveling on the road yesterday, spoke about this, and there was a very loud applause from the people in the audience, the everyday Americans, because voter ID is common sense," Leavitt continued. "They want proof of citizenship in our elections, and the president feels very strongly about that."

"The House did its job, the Senate needs to do its job too," she added.

Trump complained about the lack of action in the Senate during a trip to Georgia Wednesday and suggested he is holding back on his criticism of Senate Republicans.

"The Senate is a place you send things when you want them to die," the president grumbled. "We're not going to take this any longer; we're not going to take it. I'm trying to be nice."

The House on Wednesday passed a budget framework that would set the stage for passing a $95 billion reconciliation package, including $10 billion for election-related reforms, but the measure has an uncertain future in the Senate.

Thune said Thursday he doesn't have 50 GOP votes right now to advance the House-passed budget measure.

"As I've said before, you got to get to 50, and I can't count to 50 right now on a budget resolution," the Republican leader said when asked about the House-passed budget resolution.

"We'll see. We'll have those conversations with the conference. The priority is funding the government. To me that's the thing we got to keep our eyes on," he added.

Thune also told reporters earlier in the week that he would "hold" a House-passed budget resolution in the Senate to use as a possible vehicle to pass a short-term government funding resolution in September.

title: Ready to 'conquer Earth' - Joshua embraces comeback spotlight
description: Anthony Joshua appears relaxed and encourages a sizeable Albanian contingent, supporting his opponent, to bring their "energy" for his comeback fight on Saturday.
fullText:
A relaxed Anthony Joshua laughed with reporters, thanked fans for making the trip and even encouraged a sizeable Albanian contingent to bring their "energy" for the British heavyweight's comeback fight in Saudi Arabia on Saturday.

Joshua, 36, faces Albanian puncher Kristian Prenga in his first fight since the car crash in Nigeria last December which claimed the lives of two of his closest friends and members of his team, Sina Ghami and Latif 'Latz' Ayodele.

Victory would move Joshua to the brink of an all-British heavyweight showdown with Tyson Fury, as long as 'The Gypsy King' also wins his warm-up fight against Mariusz Wach in Thailand on Friday.

"We've been working since January, working hard, keeping my head down and aligning all my stars," Joshua said at Thursday's news conference in Jeddah.

"I'm fully focused. I understand there's a big picture. The goal is to conquer the universe, right? But first we must conquer Earth. And this is my target here."

Pre-fight Joshua not ready for death of friends to sink in

The 10 Fights That Shaped Anthony Joshua

Fury lighter than opponent for first time in 15 years

The Watford fighter stopped Youtuber-turned-boxer Jake Paul in Miami just 10 days before the fatal crash.

But Joshua has not beaten an established heavyweight since getting the better of Otto Wallin in December 2023.

Prenga, now based in New Jersey, has won 20 pro bouts by knockout and lost once, but the 35-year-old has not faced anyone close to Joshua's level.

His manager Keith Sullivan described the contest as a "real-life Rocky moment", while Prenga predicted he would win by knockout.

Joshua smiled when asked whether he believed that was possible.

"Can he knock me out? I really don't believe it," Joshua said. "But only God knows that."

Joshua has often presented as a guarded figure during fight weeks, frequently sitting through media duties and offering little.

This time, he appeared noticeably more relaxed. The headphones he regularly wears were gone, he exchanged jokes with the host about wearing a blazer in the sweltering Jeddah heat, and he saluted supporters as he took his seat on stage.

"I've been in the ring with some of the quickest heavyweights, some of the fittest heavyweights. I've fought some of the strongest heavyweights," said Joshua, whose career record shows 29 victories - 26 inside the distance - and four defeats.

"I've fought contenders. I've fought champions. I've been knocked down. I've been knocked out. I stand strong today and I'm ready for the challenge one million per cent."

When an Albanian supporter weighed in with a shout of "You ain't ready", Joshua replied: "We're going to find out on Saturday and I want to hear your voice shouting. I want that energy in the ring."

Talk inevitably turned to Fury, who is expected to fly to Jeddah from Thailand on Friday.

Promoter Eddie Hearn said there are "many, many more chapters to write" in Joshua's career, beginning with the long-awaited meeting with Fury, which is mooted to take place later this year.

Joshua accepted the media's interest but gently batted questions away.

"We don't want to talk about Tyson Fury," he said. "Respectfully, I've done all the media I can about what the future holds. Our focus is [Prenga] and his team.

"Let's focus on the job. Anyone can be there on Saturday. The more tickets sold, the better."
