title: Super Giants beat Spirit despite Livingstone flurry
description: Manchester Super Giants beat London Spirit by seven runs in The Hundred at Lord's, as Liam Livingstone's big-hitting effort falls just short.
fullText:
The Hundred men's competition, Lord's

Manchester Super Giants 138-9 (100 balls): Seifert 67 (45); Livingstone 2-14

London Spirit 131-6 (100 balls): Livingstone 69 (40); Atkinson 2-15

Manchester Super Giants won by seven runs

Manchester Super Giants beat London Spirit by seven runs in The Hundred at Lord's as Liam Livingstone's big-hitting innings came in vain.

The home captain's 40-ball knock of 69 from number four, preceded by bowling figures of 2-14 in Super Giants' innings, kept his side in contention as they chased 139.

Coming in after James Rew was dismissed for a two-ball duck, Livingstone put on 37 with Kiran Carlson (19) and 68 with Dewald Brevis.

South Africa international Brevis played junior partner, scoring 22 off 23 balls, before being caught and bowled by Gus Atkinson, who took 2-15, in the penultimate set.

Needing 12 off the final five balls, Livingstone faced England seamer Sonny Baker, who had conceded 25 runs from his first 15 deliveries.

Livingstone had already struck five sixes in his innings and was looking to go big again.

It was 23-year-old Baker who came out on top, though, conceding just four more runs, bowling Livingstone and then having James Coles caught from the final delivery of the match as Spirit finished on 131-6.

Having been put in to bat earlier, Super Giants captain Aiden Markram made a slow start, scoring one off 10 by the time he was trapped lbw by Adam Milne, but opening partner Tim Seifert fared rather better.

The New Zealander struck 67 from 45 deliveries, sharing partnerships of 49 and 40 with Jos Buttler (16) and Heinrich Klaasen (17).

However, no further batters made it to double figures, with Spirit losing seven wickets for 35 runs as they tallied 138-9.

Seifert's wicket was the seventh to fall when he was caught at mid-wicket by David Willey off Coles, but he had ultimately helped set a winning total for the visitors.

Birmingham Phoenix will take on Trent Rockets at Edgbaston on Friday, with the women's game starting at 15:00 BST and the men's at 18:30.

title: Italy confirm Ancelotti approach before Guardiola
description: Italy held discussions with Carlo Ancelotti to become their national team manager before opening talks with Pep Guardiola.
fullText:
Italy approached Carlo Ancelotti to become their national team manager before holding talks with Pep Guardiola.

The Azzurri have been without a head coach since Gennaro Gattuso resigned in April following the country's failure to qualify for a third successive World Cup.

Earlier this week, Italian Football Federation (FIGC) president Giovanni Malago said talks had been held with Guardiola, who left Manchester City this summer after 10 years, and "exceptions" could be made for his salary.

But on Thursday, FIGC technical director Paolo Maldini said Italy first approached their former player Ancelotti, who is currently in charge of Brazil.

"We cannot hide the fact that we also spoke to Carlo before talking to Pep," said Maldini during a news conference.

"Frankly, it seemed right to start with those we considered the best in the world."

Ancelotti, a former team-mate of Maldini for both Italy and AC Milan, took charge of Brazil in 2025 and extended his contract in May until the 2030 World Cup.

And despite a World Cup last-16 exit at the hands of Norway, the Brazilian Football Confederation confirmed the 67-year-old will remain in charge of the five-time world champions.

Maldini, a former defender who earned 126 caps for Italy, said FIGC would ideally appoint a coach by the end of the week but the priority was getting the right man.

Italian news outlet La Gazzetta dello Sport reported, external Guardiola was seeking an annual salary of 20m euros (£17m), which is double what Italy was offering.

"We honestly cannot provide any updates on what is happening at the moment," added Maldini when asked about the report.

Guardiola, 55, led City to six Premier League titles, three FA Cups, five League Cups and the Champions League during a trophy-laden decade in England.

Former Italy managers Antonio Conte and Roberto Mancini, as well as former Azzurri midfielder Andrea Pirlo, have also been linked with the job.

title: Defoe leaves Woking after 'eye-opening' first managerial role
description: Former England and Tottenham striker Jermain Defoe has described his first managerial job as an "eye-opening experience" following his departure from National League side Woking after just four months and nine matches in charge.
fullText:
Former England striker Jermain Defoe has described his first managerial job as an "eye-opening experience" following his exit from National League side Woking after just four months and six league games in charge.

The 43-year-old was named the replacement for Neal Ardley in March and won two of those six matches to guide the club to a 10th-placed finish last season.

But on Thursday, Woking confirmed Defoe's stint had come to an abrupt end after they "mutually agreed to part ways with immediate effect" - two weeks before the opening match of the new campaign against Sutton United.

"Unfortunately, circumstances have ultimately made it impossible for me to continue in the position," Defoe said in an Instagram post.

"I am extremely proud of the work undertaken during my time at the club and genuinely felt that we were building something special.

"My time at Woking FC has been an eye-opening managerial experience and one that will undoubtedly make me stronger for the next challenge in my career."

Defoe's ninth and final game in charge of Woking was a 1-0 defeat by Braintree Town in one of three pre-season friendlies on Tuesday.

He scored 305 goals in 763 senior appearances for clubs including West Ham, Tottenham, Portsmouth and Sunderland before retiring in 2022.

Defoe, who struck 20 times in 57 international appearances, was part of an interim coaching staff at Rangers in 2021 and then worked in the Spurs academy, before leaving in 2024 to pursue a full-time managerial role.

Defoe said just a few days ago he was excited and relishing the new season.

So this is a hugely surprising end to a partnership that seemed to be heading in the right direction.

The club's fans had been buzzing since his arrival, with many of the squad assembled this summer by Defoe and director of football Jody Brown joining Woking because of the former England striker.

But the reference in Defoe's statement, external to owner Todd Johnson - thanking him "for giving me the opportunity to manage the club" - but a lack of one for Brown could reveal a lot about the departure.

Defoe had impressed the club with his coaching and work on the training field, so all eyes point towards off the pitch for the reasons he said made it "impossible" to continue after 116 days in charge.

And where next for Defoe? This was meant to be his breakthrough in management. As for Woking, it's hardly an ideal time to be looking for a new head coach.

title: King's Tardis entrance as Commonwealth Games begin
description: King Charles III emerges from a Tardis and a Loch Ness Monster takes the stage at the Commonwealth Games opening ceremony in Glasgow.
fullText:
King Charles III, the Loch Ness Monster and thousands of athletes and Para-athletes took centre stage as the 2026 Commonwealth Games officially began with the opening ceremony.

King Charles and Queen Camilla were greeted by cycling great Sir Chris Hoy as they arrived into Glasgow's Hydro Arena in Doctor Who's Tardis – a reference to the blue police boxes seen around the city.

After taking their seats alongside new Prime Minister Andy Burnham, who was attending his first major engagement since replacing Sir Keir Starmer, athletes from the 74 competing nations and territories entered the arena.

Those from Botswana were the first to emerge, with athletes dressed in the colours of their nations, including Ghana's red, green, yellow and black stripes, South Africa in Springbok-style rugby jerseys, and Barbados in blue and yellow combined with tartan scarves.

One of the louder cheers came for the Falklands Islands – in the news recently following Argentina's victory over England in the football World Cup semi-finals – and another followed when the Samoa team emerged and performed a haka in front of the dignitaries in the Royal Box.

But nothing compared to the noise which greeted the arrival of the Scotland team, who entered the arena last in their white shirts and kilts and lifted many in the capacity crowd from their seats.

A large Loch Ness Monster representation then emerged, before, backed by bagpipers and drummers, a rousing rendition of Flower of Scotland followed.

The next section of the evening featured theatrical performances – a show billed as "a love letter to Glasgow and the Commonwealth".

It began with a performance by Scottish folk and country singer Nathan Evans and a dance under a replica of Glasgow Central Station's iconic clock.

The saying "Meet me under the clock" is famous in these parts.

Singer Callum Beattie's rendition of Baccara's Yes Sir, I Can Boogie – the 1970s disco tune recently adopted as Scotland's unofficial anthem - had the Scottish athletes dancing in their seats.

Held indoors in front of about 10,000 people, this was a more intimate ceremony than the last time Glasgow hosted the Commonwealth Games, when a 40,000-strong crowd witnessed the opening of the Games in 2014 at Celtic Park.

There was a point, after the Australian state of Victoria withdrew as hosts in 2023, when some thought these Games would not happen. Glasgow stepped in at short notice to ensure otherwise.

There are eight fewer sports involved compared to 2014, given the short build-up, and venues from 12 years ago have been repurposed.

But Commonwealth Sport president Donald Rukare, wearing a tartan kilt in the colours of his home country Uganda, said this edition has been made possible by the "extraordinary legacy of 2014 and by a city that once again welcomes us with warm hearts and open arms".

As it happened: Dancing, teacakes & King Charles star in vibrant opening ceremony

George Black, chair of Glasgow 2026, added: "When the future of these Games was uncertain, Glasgow didn't ask: 'Can we?'

"We asked: 'When do we get started?'

"People across this city should be incredibly proud of what has been achieved over the past 18 months.

"Venues opened their doors. Communities opened their hearts.

"Businesses, artists, schools, volunteers, partners, neighbours and the people of Glasgow came together with one shared belief: that the Commonwealth Games matter and that the world deserves to experience them."

Scottish singer KT Tunstall's rendition of No Scotland No Party – another anthem of the Tartan Army this summer – later in the evening was fitting on this occasion.

Prior to that, the King's Baton was brought into the arena while singer Nina Nesbitt performed another Scottish classic, the Proclaimers' classic I'm Gonna Be (500 Miles).

The King used his speech to stress the importance of preventing plastic pollution in oceans and waterways.

"I extend my warmest gratitude to the city of Glasgow and all those have made this event possible," he said.

"As we share our love of sport and our planet, let us build a harmonious future for our families and each other."

The sporting action runs across the next 10 days before the Games conclude with the closing ceremony on Sunday, 2 August, back at the Hydro.

The first medals are set to be won in Para-powerlifting in the early evening of Friday.

title: Sweden's far right party says it won the arguments. Now it wants a share of power
description: After championing huge policy shifts in Sweden by propping up a minority centre-right government for four years, the far-right Sweden Democrats say
fullText:
STOCKHOLM, July 23 (Reuters) - After championing huge policy shifts in Sweden by propping up a minority centre-right government for four years, the far-right Sweden Democrats say they have won the political arguments and now deserve a share of power for themselves.

But it is a demand that could prove a step too far for voters in an election in September, and potentially ‌bring down Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson. The prime minister, who once told a Holocaust survivor he would never cooperate with the Sweden Democrats, now says he has lifted those "red lines".

Sweden, the stalwart of European ‌socialism for generations, has undergone a startling rightward shift in policy and public opinion in recent years, especially over immigration in a country where one person in five was born abroad.

The number of asylum seekers last year was down 97% from the peak a decade ago. One ​of Europe's most welcoming immigration systems has become one of its most stringent: newcomers face employment restrictions, language and culture tests, and reduced welfare payouts. Immigrants who break the law can be swiftly expelled, and others are offered payments to leave voluntarily.

The changes were all championed by the Sweden Democrats, who spent the past four years providing 73 votes in the 349-seat Riksdag to support the centre-right government from outside Kristersson's coalition.

Party leader Jimmie Akesson says that arrangement has run its course, and his party must now become a full-blown member of the coalition, controlling most of the key ministries in the cabinet if the right wins the vote.

"It's been a clear demand from us ahead of this election. Either we are ‌in government or we are in opposition," Akesson told Reuters in an interview. "We ⁠have shown that we can cooperate, it has gone better than most people expected."

After the election, the Sweden Democrats expect to control at least three of the most powerful ministries — justice, defence, foreign affairs or finance, Akesson said.

"I think that is reasonable if you look at the balance of power within our bloc," he said. He would allow Kristersson to ⁠remain as prime minister, he added: "I can do without travelling around the world and shaking hands."

The Sweden Democrats were once ostracised by all of Sweden's mainstream parties, including Kristersson's main centre-right Moderates.

But at a joint press conference with Akesson in April, the prime minister officially lifted the taboo: "We have done away with red lines that can limit our cooperation and want to form a stable majority government after the election," he announced. "That's a strength for Sweden."

Voters so far appear unconvinced. A major poll in ​June ​showed support for the governing bloc including the Sweden Democrats at just 42.6%, with the loosely aligned centre-left opposition led by ​the Social Democrats receiving 55.2%.

"Kristersson is caught between a rock and a hard place. He ‌can't form a government without the Sweden Democrats, but they might also scare off too many voters," said Gothenburg University Political Science Professor Jonas Hinnfors.

Winning Kristersson's public acceptance was "a monumental journey" for the Sweden Democrats, Hinnfors said. "They weren't just a small party, they were also a taboo party, a pariah party that no one wanted to touch with pliers."

The Sweden Democrats acknowledge that some members had roots in neo-Nazism and white supremacy when the party was founded in the 1980s, but say such figures have since been weeded out.

Akesson, 47, joined the party in the 1990s and became party leader in 2005. Since then, he has pushed relentlessly for mainstream acceptance. A keyboardist in a Viking rock band, he cultivates an informal persona and is usually seen in a suit without a tie.

The rise of the Sweden Democrats has forced other parties to alter their positions, especially on immigration and crime. In 2015, when ‌more than a million asylum seekers entered Europe mainly from Syria, Sweden proudly took in more per capita than any other EU ​country, with Prime Minister Stefan Lofven, a Social Democrat, saying his Europe "didn't build walls".

Now, with policy having shifted and the numbers of ​new immigrants having already plummeted, the Sweden Democrats' focus has shifted toward demands that the existing foreign-born ​population espouse Swedish values, language and customs.

The party calls for banning traditional Islamic dress for women, tearing down mosques that preach Islamic radicalism, increasing expulsions and raising financial incentives for those ‌who leave voluntarily.

"We want the complete abolition of multiculturalism," said Mattias Karlsson, seen as ​the party's chief ideologist.

While opinion polls suggest support for the ​Sweden Democrats remains fairly steady at around a fifth of the electorate, the agreement to allow them into a future government has hurt other parties in Kristersson's coalition.

The Liberals, who now provide the ruling coalition with 16 seats, saw several high-profile representatives quit and the party's support fall below the 4% threshold needed to win seats.

The Sweden Democrats' "values are as far from mine as possible. To be in government ​with them is unthinkable," said Liberal lawmaker Malin Sjoberg Hogrell, who quit her party's ‌governing board and announced she would not seek re-election to parliament.

Opposition parties say Kristersson's deal with the Sweden Democrats means the only way to keep the far right out of power is ​now to vote for the left.

"Kristersson would be the weakest prime minister in Sweden's history," Social Democrat leader Magdalena Andersson told a news conference. "That government would only last until the day Jimmie ​Akesson decides it's time for him to become prime minister."

title: Megan Rapinoe says efforts to bar trans athletes from women's sports are 'attack on women in society'
description: Megan Rapinoe appeared on "The View" to argue that transgender athlete bans are a broader attack on women's empowerment and confidence overall.
fullText:
Former U.S. Women's National Team star Megan Rapinoe said Tuesday that efforts to bar transgender athletes from competing in women's sports amount to "an attack on women in society," arguing the debate extends far beyond questions of fairness in athletic competition.

Appearing on ABC's "The View," Rapinoe dismissed arguments that restrictions on transgender athletes are primarily about protecting girls' and women's sports. Instead, she argued, opponents are using the issue as part of a broader campaign against women's empowerment.

"They're going after trans people in sports, and they're using this as a wedge issue because we love sports. And, you know, we don't want our little girls to be unsafe in sports. And it's like, 'Unsafe from who? But okay,'" Rapinoe said.

California Billionaire Tom Steyer Defends Trans Athletes In High School Sports As Governor's Race Heats Up

"But now we're using this as like, an overarching thing, when really what we're attacking women, we're saying, 'If you look like this as a woman, you can play, if you look like this as a woman.' There's a bill in Washington happening right now that's going to require all girls, just like full stop, to go through a sex test, an invasive, like examining their genitals to see if they're a woman," Rapinoe said.

Initiative IL26-638, according to the House Republican Caucus, would "prohibit students it defines as 'biologically male' from competing in certain school athletic activities intended for female students only. It would require verification of biological sex by students' healthcare providers."

Trans Athletes Drop Lawsuit To Gain Access To Girls' Sports In New Hampshire After Scotus Ruling

"They don't like women in sports because it helps us with confidence and power," Rapinoe said. "And we see so many women in the C-suite played elite level sports. It's just great for you physically and all of it. But it's an attack on women in society."

Wnba Player Faces Backlash Over Op-ed Criticizing Ioc For Policy To Protect Women's Sports

In a recent article in Marie Claire entitled, "Anti-Trans Legislation Isn't About 'Protecting Girls'—It's About Distracting Us From What Matters," Rapinoe wrote,

"The truth is, trans people have been competing in sports for decades, and will continue to participate as this smoke screen from bad faith legislators and anti-LGBT groups cloak the real damage being done. As with so many other manufactured issues we fall for, women and girls will suffer the most."

Rapinoe did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital's request for comment.

title: ‘Call us whatever the hell you want’: Mark Zuckerberg just launched an AI optimism blitz using nostalgia to sell Meta’s AI future amid backlash
description: Marketing experts say Meta invokes Facebook-era optimism while leaving recent AI lawsuits and product backlash unaddressed.
fullText:
In a week where an AI model went rogue and broke out of a secure test environment—and in a year where seemingly all we're hearing is how AI can displace large swaths of the workforce—it may come as a surprise when one of the tech's most prominent voices is pushing an optimistic view of the tech, joining the many in his field who have recently walked back comments of how it could negatively impact workers.

What recent controversies is Meta facing with AI?

How effective is nostalgic marketing for AI concerns?

Why is Meta using nostalgia to market AI?

How are AI leaders walking back job displacement fears?

"As we enter this next wave with AI, we continue to believe the future is for everyone," Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg wrote as the caption to a short video on Thursday, harkening back to the company's early years of tech optimism.

"Call us optimists, call us dreamers, call us whatever the hell you want, but we're betting on people, and we like those odds," the voiceover in the video said.

The video featured montages of people using Instagram, Whatsapp, FaceBook and Meta's Ray-Ban glasses to film touching moments and connect with loved ones, with the backdrop of concerts and snowboarding and laughing children set to calm music as the voiceover framed Meta's AI efforts as doubling down on betting on people, just like the company did 22 years ago with FaceBook.

Marketing experts told Fortune that the Meta video's reliance on nostalgic imagery seemed like a way to redirect attention from AI concerns and to buffer against growing AI fears.

"They're showing people having a great time, but they're not really responding to what the fundamental issue is, which is how is AI going to impact me when I start dealing with your platform when you integrate all of that technology into it," Mara Einstein, a media studies professor at CUNY's Queens College, told Fortune. "The problem is the AI technology is not the same as previous versions of social media that they have used to connect people in the past."

The sentiment was echoed by Ana Babic Rosario, a marketing professor at the University of Denver's Daniels College of Business, who pointed to Meta's more recent struggles as reason why Meta might want to draw nostalgia to the past. Last week, Meta employees sued the company for allegedly using AI in the company's recent 8,000 layoffs that the employees claim disproportionately targeted workers on medical, parental or family leave. Employees at Meta's Applied AI unit also compared working conditions at the company to a "gulag" and the tasks "soul crushing." The company also scrapped an AI-generated images pilot on Instagram, and is facing a lawsuit on its Ray-Ban Meta glasses.

title: The roaring 20s: how the current decade revolutionised cinema
description:
Pre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next? 
Cinema has always been a medium of research and development. At any given point, the industry is searching for yet another breakthrough that can drive it into the future. Synchronised sound in the 1920s.
fullText:
Pre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next?

Cinema has always been a medium of research and development. At any given point, the industry is searching for yet another breakthrough that can drive it into the future. Synchronised sound in the 1920s. Technicolor in the 1930s. The invention of the modern blockbuster in the 1970s.

Conversely, the consensus is that, if the 2020s has a defining trend, then it is one of decline. The theatrical experience is under attack from all angles: streaming, YouTube, a once-in-a-century health crisis that shut cinemas down entirely. And yet, if you look closer, this has actually been one of the most innovative decades that cinema has ever seen. Whether down to technology or desperation, the film industry has spent these last few years figuring out new ways to make films that simply could not have existed even at the turn of the decade.

Currently, the most notable of these is the way that cinema has become a conversation with YouTube. Back in 2005, when Jawed Karim uploaded the first video (the 19-second Me at the Zoo) to the platform, YouTube felt like a novelty. It was cheap and cheerful, a collection of cat videos and people falling over. How things have changed. Not only has it become the largest streaming platform in the world, but the first generation of adults who grew up with YouTube has come of age.

As we speak, the seventh and eighth biggest films of 2026 have been directed by people who learned their trade making content for YouTube. Obsession (worldwide gross: $442m) was directed by Curry Barker, a 26-year-old who dropped out of film school to make YouTube videos and TikToks. Meanwhile, Backrooms (worldwide gross: $384m) is even more explicitly internet-inspired. Directed by the then 20-year-old Kane Parsons, the film is based on a series of shorts he began uploading to YouTube in 2022, which in turn were inspired by some creepypasta (web-based horror content) that has been floating around online since 2019.

A decade ago, studios would have got this disastrously wrong. Ask them to adapt a YouTube video and they would have hired Michael Bay and Rihanna to make David After Dentist: The Motion Picture; it would have baffled anyone who didn’t watch YouTube – and appalled anyone who did. This is the first time that the industry has trusted natives to make the films they want to make. Clearly, given the enormous financial returns, it will not be the last. Steven Spielberg is now producing Alex Kister’s The Mandela Catalogue, based on a terrifying analogue horror series that has run on YouTube since 2021.

This would also be a good time to mention the rise of the copyright expiry movie. In 1998, the Copyright Term Extension Act was passed in the US, which extended protection to works to 95 years after they were first published. (In the UK, the rules are different; copyright extends to 70 years after the creator’s death.) This effectively meant that two decades went by without anything going out of copyright. When things finally caught up in 2019, freeing up anything made in 1923, there was a goldrush.

Now, let it be said that it wasn’t a particularly good goldrush. First out of the blocks was 2023’s Winnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey, a low-rent slasher movie whose only original thought was to have Winnie-the-Pooh in it. Then came The Mouse Trap and Screamboat (both based on Steamboat Willie, the first element of the Mickey Mouse character to have lost copyright protection), along with Popeye the Slayer Man and Bambi: The Reckoning. Without exception, these films are cheap, exploitative and burn out their premise long before the end credits – but more are to come.

Next year, if you can wait that long, will see the release of something called Poohniverse: Monsters Assemble, which will star – deep breath – Winnie-the-Pooh, Tigger, Bambi, Peter Pan, Tinker Bell, Pinocchio, Sleeping Beauty and the Cheshire Cat. It is also worth pointing out that King Kong enters the public domain in 2029, which will be brilliant news for anyone who has been longing to see a low-rent horror movie starring an unusually large gorilla.

Now, here is where I may lose you, because I am about to name Sonic the Hedgehog as the most important movie of the decade. Please bear with me. One of the most pervasive trends of this decade has been the way that, for good or ill, audiences have hijacked movies to their own ends. The biggest example is obviously Barbenheimer, in which Barbie and Oppenheimer were released on the same day, kickstarting a phenomenon where people bought tickets to see one straight after the other. Nobody in the film industry expected this, but the publicity from this grassroots celebration drove receipts of both films through the roof.

The flipside to this is Morbius, a terrible film that inspired no end of ironic online mockery – but what was hilarious was that Sony mistook the irony for sincere support, and rereleased the film on 1,000 screens to cash in. It goes without saying that nobody watched it. Also, think of all the extra teenagers who bought tickets for Minions: The Rise of Gru, purely so they could watch dressed in suits and babble nonsense to participate in the Gentleminions trend, or the people who went to see A Minecraft Movie exclusively to scream and chuck stuff about when Jack Black said the words “chicken jockey”.

No film, arguably, has benefited from fan reaction quite like Sonic the Hedgehog. The first we saw of it was a nightmarish trailer featuring an unrecognisable main character. Sonic had bizarrely human proportions, as well as tiny eyes and teeth that looked as if they had been stolen from someone’s mouth while they slept. It was awful, and the internet wasted no time in saying that it was awful. In times gone by, studios would have simply taken the hit and released the film as quietly as possible, writing it off as another godawful video game adaptation destined to die on impact. But this time, producers listened. The trailer was released on Tuesday. By Thursday, the director had tweeted that the character would be completely redesigned. Six months after that, New Sonic was unveiled, and fans were delighted.

With a character that people could actually stand to look at, Sonic the Hedgehog went on to become the sixth biggest film of 2020. Nobody saw this coming. Until Sonic, video game adaptations were universally written off as the lowest form of cinema. Think of the dismal live action Super Mario Bros movie, or Mark Wahlberg’s baffling Max Payne, or Uwe Boll’s irredeemable Postal. About 46 English language video game adaptations were released between 1993 and 2019, and they made a combined total of $5.4bn. But A Minecraft Movie made close to a $1bn on its own. The animated Super Mario Movie released in 2023 ended the year second only to Barbie. At time of writing, The Super Mario Galaxy Movie is the biggest film of 2026 so far worldwide. By demonstrating to other film-makers how to give people what they actually want to see, Sonic has turned the video game adaptation into a licence to print money.

As you will agree, this makes Sonic the Hedgehog by far the most important film of the decade. And the future will only bear this out further, once Mr Beast signs on to direct Sonic the Hedgehog 5 and someone makes a rubbish horror film about a blue hedgehog, as soon as the rights lapse in 2087.

title: Elon Musk says he got ‘carried away’ with Trump – but still holds on to contentious political views

fullText:
In interview, billionaire says instead of running ‘Doge’ he should’ve ‘worked on my companies’ and shares extreme predictions

Elon Musk has admitted he became too consumed by politics during his volatile alliance with Donald Trump and his brief appointment as the president’s financial axman, running the so-called “department of government efficiency” (Doge).

The world’s richest person, and briefly its first trillionaire after his SpaceX company floated on the New York stock exchange in June, made the confession in a wide-ranging interview with the Economist published on Thursday. He shared his positions on subjects from foreign wars to AI, and made a number of eye-opening predictions for the future.

“I got carried away, frankly,” Musk said of his relationship with the president, whom he helped get elected in 2024 with $200m in financial backing, then served during his controversial tenure as head of Doge, overseeing $150bn in “harmful” budget cuts and forcing tens of thousands of people out of federal jobs.

The owner of Tesla did not expand on the statement, but appeared cognizant of the impact of his dalliance with the levers of power in Washington DC.

In December, he offered in a podcast with Katie Miller, wife of Trump’s White House adviser Stephen Miller, that his wrecking-ball approach to the federal government had been only partially successful, and that given the opportunity he probably wouldn’t do it again.

“I think instead of doing Doge, I would have basically worked on my companies,” he said at the time.

Talking to the Economist, however, he appeared to relish the attention his political involvement had garnered, and the influence he believed he exerted. “People don’t realize that what I’m saying will come to pass,” he said, the outlet referring to Musk – at least in his own mind – as a modern-day Cassandra.

The interview, a 90-minute conversation for the publication’s video platform The Insider, recorded at a Tesla factory in Texas, was Musk’s first since the SpaceX flotation, in which his personal wealth briefly topped $1.25tn before dropping back to a relatively penurious $750bn.

He reflected on moving from “exhilaration to terror” in a single day, but was not referring to the SpaceX share price. Instead, he was talking about artificial intelligence, which he believes will ultimately render work by humans “optional”.

AI systems and robots will eventually handle most digital and physical jobs, he said, and the merger of his own AI venture with SpaceX will allow him to develop systems to perform cognitive tasks such as software programming while Tesla manufactures humanoids.

Musk, the Economist said, plans to power his AI venture with datacenters in space, which experts have warned will be “catastrophic” for Earth.

Within five years, Musk said, AI intelligence systems may surpass the sum of all human intelligence. Within 10 years, robots in the workplace will help usher in an era of such “amazing abundance” that money will become meaningless.

It was, however, Musk’s political views, and predictions, that were perhaps the most contentious parts of the interview. The UK, a country he has not visited for years, but whose politics, especially on immigration and race, have become a recent obsession, will become engulfed in a civil war within 20 years, he claimed.

Asked if he was anti-Muslim, Musk attacked the questioner, a policy of diversion similar to that which has served Trump well, and which he has employed previously, notably calling the Guardian “irrelevant” when it decided to withdraw from his “toxic” social media platform X.

“I’m against rape and murder. I’m against the imposition of rules and laws that are contrary to what we’ve come to accept in the west,” he said. “It’s a crying shame that traditional media, like you, don’t recognize this.”

He described the media’s characterization of Europe’s far right as “false, misleading and nonsense”, and argued that it was made up of “normal people” seeking safe cities, secure borders and sensible spending, the Economist said.

In an analysis piece accompanying the interview, the outlet acknowledged that Musk was a divisive figure. “His political views, disseminated to his 240m followers on X, strike many as plain-speaking and strike many more, including The Economist, as plainly bigoted,” it wrote.

“But he is also one of a handful of men who are pioneering AI and who thereby have an outsize influence on its trajectory. When he speaks, he reflects the debates they are having. His datacentres in space could power AI’s future.

“For all his political polemics, he has a record of being right about technology in fields such as electric cars, rockets and satellite communications that confounded other engineers and entrepreneurs.”

Another Musk prediction: after AI destroys jobs, there may need to be a large-scale wealth redistribution to create “universal high income” for humans.

He said he would be willing to pay “trillions in tax” then, conversely, argued that money will become irrelevant inside 10 years. In which eventuality, he said, governments should “issue people cheques”, because, he said, amid an infinite supply of goods and services produced by machines, deflation will be a bigger problem than inflation.

He also shared thoughts about the Ukraine war, in which his Starlink satellites have been used to help that country’s military against Russia’s aggression. Despite this, Musk said any peace deal should include territorial gains for Russia.

The interview concluded with an assessment by Musk of his achievements and ambitious current projects, in AI and in space, a field in which he has also made a number of bold predictions. He said in 2024 that humans could set foot on Mars by 2028 (currently Nasa is targeting 2028 for a return to the moon, which many analysts think is unlikely).

The universe, Musk said, according to the Economist, could be a computer simulation created by aliens. “The things I’m doing are so preposterous that it’s hard to believe they’re real,” he said.

title: Morning Bid: Magnificent cash burn
description: July 23 (Reuters) - What matters in U. and global markets today , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets There was a familiar playbook in response to Alphabet's earnings beat and capital expenditure boost overnight - its stock fell and chip stocks rose.
fullText:
What matters in U.S. and global markets today

There was a familiar playbook in response to Alphabet's earnings beat and capital expenditure boost overnight - its stock fell and chip stocks rose.

The hyperscaler posted more than 80% growth ‌in its key cloud business, beating the Street, but raised its capex estimates yet again - with AI-linked investments this year now set to top $200 ‌billion.

Why did Alphabet's stock fall despite beating earnings?

What's driving oil prices toward $98 per barrel?

How are rising AI investments affecting tech companies?

How might ECB policy respond to inflation pressures?

I'll get into that and more below.

But first, check out my latest column on how the sheer scale of the U.S. K-shaped economy explains its resilience.

And listen to the latest episode of the ​Morning Bid daily podcast, where we discuss Alphabet's soaring AI bill and the fresh inflation headache facing the ECB.

Partly on some disquiet about delays in its latest Gemini AI model, as well as the rising cash burn, markets shrugged at the headlines and knocked Alphabet's stock down 3% ahead of today's bell.

Lapping up the higher spend, however, chip-heavy South Korean stocks jumped more than 4% on Thursday. But it ‌wasn't all good news in the chip world either, ⁠with Europe's STMicroelectronics dropping 14% first thing on a slight miss in its earnings.

Stateside, Tesla stock also flubbed and lost 4% on Wednesday after it reported its first negative free cash flow in over two years. Intel's earnings are due out later today, ⁠a test for the U.S. chipmaking giant as its shares remain up nearly three times so far this year, even after tumbling from a record high in late June.

Wall Street futures were down before the bell on Thursday, while European shares also edged lower as tech stocks there slipped, led by chipmaker STMicro after its below-expectations results.

More broadly, oil climbed ​further ​toward $98 per barrel overnight amid the raging conflict in the Gulf and the new shipping hiatus ​in the Red Sea, as Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis targeted Saudi ‌oil tankers and shipping data showed tankers changing course.

The resurgent oil prices are pressuring oil and natural gas futures, as well as interest rate and bond markets in turn. Short-term U.S. Treasury yields rose to their highest in around 17 months on Thursday.

With the European Central Bank meeting today, the inflation impact from spiraling natural gas prices as winter storage starts to get rebuilt will mean the signals are likely to be hawkish.
